fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

PARKER J:

The applications before the Court relate to parenting and property settlement proceedings. Each of the parties sought orders with respect to the care arrangements for their son, X, born 2021 (aged 4 at the time of the trial), and orders dividing their property between them pursuant to section 79 of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”).

BACKGROUND

The applicant, MS PRASET (“the mother” or “the wife”) was born in 1988 and was aged 37 at the time of conclusion of the trial. She works as a medical professional and resides in Adelaide, South Australia.
The respondent, MR JANEK (“the father” or “the husband”) was born in 1987 and was aged 38 at the time of conclusion of the trial. He works as a tradesperson. He presently resides in Town J, South Australia, hundreds of kilometres from Adelaide, though he has indicated that he may move to Adelaide in future.
The parties commenced their relationship in South Australia in 2012. They commenced cohabitation in Darwin, Northern Territory, in January 2014 and were married in October 2019. In 2020, the parties returned to South Australia and moved to City K. The parties separated on 2 September 2021 after a period of cohabitation of approximately seven years and nine months. A divorce order became final in October 2023.
X is the only child of the parties’ relationship.
The wife gave birth to her second child in 2025, and X thus has a sibling in her household. She is in a committed relationship with, but does not cohabit with, her second child’s father.

THE HUSBAND’S ADJOURNMENT APPLICATION

On 1 October 2025, the husband sought an adjournment of the trial, which was listed for 8 October 2025, on the basis that his solicitors had withdrawn from acting for him. By virtue of the existence of an order made pursuant to section 102NA of the Act, in the absence of legal representation, the husband would not be permitted to cross-examine the wife. The adjournment application was opposed by the wife and by the Independent Children’s Lawyer (“ICL”).
The adjournment application was refused for reasons including:
(a) The husband had, by that stage, had no less than eight different solicitors on the court record as acting for him over the course of the proceedings, a number of whom had withdrawn very shortly after commencing to represent him. It appeared that the husband was unable or unwilling to maintain enduring professional relationships with his legal representatives. As a result, it could not be assumed that an adjournment would have resulted in the husband being represented at trial. As it transpired, the husband tendered documents demonstrating that he had been represented by an additional solicitor in the lead up to the trial,[1] and a tenth solicitor commenced acting for the husband and then withdrew two weeks later during a period when the trial was subsequently adjourned part-heard after being unable to be completed within the time allocated to it.

(b) The application was made against the backdrop of a history of repeated and ongoing failures on the husband’s part to comply with court orders and directions. This included the trial directions, which had been made, and the date for compliance with which had passed, while the husband had been legally represented.

(c) The order pursuant to section 102NA had been made on 18 September 2024, over a year earlier. Not only had he had ample time to engage legal representatives, but the husband had had three separate sets of solicitors file Notices of Address for Service followed by Notices of Ceasing to Act on his behalf between that date and the date of the adjournment application, rendering it inconceivable that he had not been made aware of the consequences of that order.

(d) An assessment that further protraction of the litigation was likely to be contrary to X’s best interests.

(e) The potential for risk to the wife and her parenting if the litigation were to continue, in light of her evidence of family violence and abusive conduct inflicted upon her by the husband.

(f) The fact that the proceedings had been commenced in March 2023, when X was an infant, and had been on foot for a period of over two and a half years, which represented more than half of X’s young life.

(g) The lack of availability of alternative trial dates for the balance of 2025 and well into 2026, meaning that any further delay would be for a significant period.

(h) The fact that the wife’s evidence would be subjected to cross-examination by counsel for the ICL, who would no doubt canvass many matters relevant to the determination of X’s best interests, albeit that the ICL could not cross-examine on the husband’s behalf nor in relation to financial matters, and this would not be a substitute for cross-examination by or on behalf of the husband.

(i) The fact that the husband had had the benefit of legal advice prior to the trial, which would assist with mitigating any prejudice arising from being self-represented.

(j) The overarching purpose of the family law practice and procedure provisions, as set out in section 95 of the Act, which includes, relevantly, to facilitate the just resolution of disputes “as quickly, inexpensively and efficiently as possible.” I did not consider that this purpose would be promoted by permitting further delay in the finalisation of the proceedings.

(k) I was not satisfied that it was in the interests of justice to grant the adjournment having regard to the factors outlined in Aon Risk Services Australia Ltd v Australian National University[2] and other applicable authorities, including but not limited to the competing interests of the parties in the present case, the interests of other litigants awaiting listings, and the need for efficiency in the allocation of judicial resources.

The following observations of Riethmuller J (sitting in the appellate jurisdiction) in Bryson & Bryson[3] are apposite:
The underlying principle is that litigants must be provided a reasonable opportunity to be heard. The application of the general principle to the variety of circumstances that confront trial judges depends upon the particular circumstances of each case. In this case, there is no complaint that there was inadequate notice of the date of the trial. It cannot be said that a party has not been afforded procedural fairness simply because the conduct of that party leaves them at a disadvantage, such as engaging representatives on a limited basis, failing to engage representatives, or failing to take advantage of the s 102NA funding scheme in a timely manner. To allow a party to have a trial adjourned (with the inevitable emotional and financial costs to the other party) simply because they have failed to arrange their representation (or make a timely application pursuant to the s 102NA scheme) or have only limited (or no) legal assistance, would impose an unfair burden upon the other party.
Prior to the commencement of the trial, the husband was given detailed information about the trial process and the applicable procedures, copies of the most relevant legislative provisions, copies of the documents relied upon by all parties, and an opportunity to consult with the duty lawyer.

MATERIAL RELIED ON

The wife relied on the following documents:
(a) Amended Initiating Application filed 17 December 2025;

(b) Trial affidavit filed 1 September 2025;

(c) Financial Statement filed 1 September 2025; and

(d) Case outline filed 1 October 2025.

The husband relied on the following documents:
(a) Amended Response to Initiating Application filed 20 October 2024;

(b) Trial affidavit filed 30 September 2025;

(c) Financial statement filed 2 October 2025; and

(d) Case outline filed 3 October 2025.

The ICL relied on a case outline filed on 3 October 2025.
Each of the parties also tendered documents throughout the course of the hearing. Although each and every aspect of the evidence relied upon by the parties has not been referred to in these reasons, I have had regard to the totality of the evidence before the court and the submissions of each of the parties.
In accordance with section 140 of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth), all findings of fact in this judgment are made on the balance of probabilities. This standard, however, is “not the measure by which an unacceptable risk of harm is to be assessed,”[4] and it has not been applied to my consideration of issues of unacceptable risk. Some of the findings in these reasons relate to conduct of a very serious nature, being findings as to family violence. I have had regard to the matters outlined in subsection 140(2) of the Evidence Act, particularly the gravity of the matters alleged, when making such findings, and am satisfied to the extent of “actual persuasion”[5] with respect to each of my findings with respect to such matters.
The husband was self-represented and prepared his own trial affidavit. He should not be criticised for lack of knowledge or expertise with respect to the drafting of court documents. Nonetheless, parts of his trial affidavit were drafted in a manner which created some considerable difficulty in discerning exactly which parts of the wife’s evidence were disputed by him and what he asserted the relevant facts actually were.[6] Noting that the court is not required to make findings in relation to all of the facts put in issue by the parties and need only determine those facts that are necessary for the determination of the issues between the parties,[7] I have done the best I can with the available evidence to identify and determine the factual disputes necessary to be resolved for the determination of the applications before the Court.

THE PARTIES AND THEIR EVIDENCE

As a consequence of the existence of the order made pursuant to section 102NA of the Act, being self-represented, the husband was not permitted to cross-examine the wife. The wife’s evidence was not wholly untested, as she was cross-examined by counsel for the ICL. Nonetheless, significant aspects of the wife’s evidence were not specifically challenged. This was entirely appropriate as it is not the role of the ICL to conduct a party’s case on that party’s behalf and the ICL is not concerned with the financial aspects of the proceedings.
Although courts are not bound to accept unchallenged evidence, rejection of such evidence must be approached with caution.[8] I am conscious that in the present case, the husband did not deliberately elect not to challenge the evidence of the wife, and nor did he elect to proceed with the trial without representation. He sought an adjournment and was not successful, at which time the option of challenging the wife’s evidence by cross-examination was not open to him. The lack of challenge to the wife’s evidence arose by operation of the statutory prohibition in section 102NA and not by way of forensic decision taken by the husband.
It cannot, therefore, be inferred that failure to challenge the wife’s evidence constitutes a concession as to its accuracy or acquiescence to its acceptance. Indeed, it was evident from the husband’s evidence and the manner in which he presented his case that he disputed many aspects of the wife’s evidence.
In those circumstances, to guard against undue prejudice to the husband, and to avoid the risk that section 102NA might operate to impede the court’s ultimate objective of determining X’s best interests and a just and equitable property settlement, the wife’s evidence has been given careful consideration and scrutiny in the context of the evidence before the Court as a whole and has not simply been accepted as a consequence of being unchallenged.
Subject to the limitations on the Court’s ability to assess the evidence of the wife in the absence of testing of that evidence by or on behalf of the husband, the wife presented as a generally reliable witness. She was calm, polite, responsive to the questions asked of her and made appropriate concessions.
The husband’s presentation in the witness box was more complex. At times he was calm and polite. At other times he was defensive, and he was occasionally facetious. Some of the answers he gave were evasive. Aspects of his evidence were incapable of acceptance. He was at times unwilling to make reasonable concessions. He presented as lacking in insight and exhibited limited capacity for self-reflection.
In addition, there were a number of instances, particularly in relation to the financial aspects of the proceedings, where the husband had failed to provide documentary evidence to support his assertions despite it being not only within his power but incumbent upon him pursuant to his obligation of disclosure to do so.[9] In all such circumstances, the benefit of any doubt arising from the state of the evidence must be afforded to the wife.[10]
The wife’s evidence has largely been accepted where it conflicts with the evidence of the husband, and I accept the evidence of the wife as set out throughout these reasons unless otherwise stated.
This is primarily as a consequence of my reservations with respect to the husband as a witness, and not as a consequence of the wife’s evidence not having been challenged. As a result of the difficulties arising with respect to the operation of section 102NA, considerably more weight has been given to those aspects of the wife’s evidence which are corroborated by other evidence, including independent records, than to evidence given by the wife which is unchallenged but uncorroborated.

PARENTING

The parties’ applications

The mother sought, in summary, that she have sole decision-making responsibility for X, that X live with her, and that the father be restrained from attending within 100 metres of a range of locations including her residence, the residences of members of her family, her workplace, and X’s childcare, kindergarten and school. She also sought injunctions restraining the father from attending within 100 metres of herself or X and from communicating with either of them.
The father sought, in summary, that X live with the mother and spend time with him every third weekend from 10.00 am on Saturday until 6.00 pm on Sunday, together with half of each school holiday period and time on special occasions. He also sought an order providing for telephone communication between X and the parent he was not with no less frequently than once every three days. Although, at the commencement of the trial, the father had sought an order for “equal shared parental responsibility” with respect to X, he reluctantly conceded at the conclusion of the trial that it was in X’s best interests for the mother to have sole responsibility for decision-making with respect to major long-term issues.
On the final day of the trial, both parties amended their applications to seek that they be at liberty to travel overseas with X during any time with him. The mother also sought that she be at liberty to obtain a passport for X.
The ICL supported the mother’s application (as amended) with respect to parenting maters in its entirety.

History of parenting arrangements

X has lived in the sole care of the mother since the parties separated when he was an infant. At the time of the parties’ separation, they were living in City K, South Australia. Following an argument between them resulting in the father temporarily leaving the former matrimonial home, the mother travelled on a pre-planned trip to Adelaide (which is her city of origin) with X shortly before the separation. She subsequently advised the father that she would not be returning to live with him and has lived in Adelaide with X since that time.
Shortly after their separation, the parties entered into a parenting plan pursuant to which X spent time with the father for a period of five hours on both Saturday and Sunday three weekends out of five, with the time on two such weekends to take place in City K and one to take place in Adelaide. This arrangement persisted for a period of approximately 12 months, and X also spent additional time with the father by agreement from time to time.
An interim family violence order was made for the protection of the mother, with the father as the respondent, in June 2022. A final order was made by consent without admissions in August 2024[11] and remained in force at the time of the trial.
The mother suspended time between X and the father in September 2022, citing concerns about the father’s mental health and X’s safety in his care.
The mother commenced the present proceedings on 3 March 2023. She sought that the father undertake a psychiatric assessment and opposed the father spending any further time with X pending that assessment.
On 10 October 2023, orders were made providing for X to spend time with the father under professional supervision at L Contact Centre (in Adelaide) for sessions of no more than two hours on no more than one occasion per week, and for them to communicate by telephone or video call twice per week. Aside from some minor variations, the visits proceeded on a monthly basis. This was at the request of the father, who indicated that he was unable to attend more frequently due to work commitments. X spent professionally supervised time with the father on a total of 14 occasions between March 2024 and September 2025. X has not seen the father since 20 September 2025 and has not spent unsupervised time with him since 18 September 2022.
X commenced attending childcare at 10 months of age and commenced kindergarten at the commencement of 2025. At the time of the trial, he was attending kindergarten two days per week and childcare two days per week and was due to commence school in mid-2026.

The family report

The Court was assisted by a family report prepared by Ms N, clinical psychologist, dated 6 March 2025. Ms N gave evidence and was cross-examined by the father, senior counsel for the mother, and counsel for the ICL. Although the father questioned her about her experience, her qualifications, and her impartiality, none of these matters were successfully undermined in cross-examination. I do not accept the submission made by the father that the family report was not a “decent, objective family report.” I accept Ms N’s evidence as to her observations and give weight to her recommendations.
Ms N recommended in her family report, in summary, that the mother have sole responsibility for decision-making with respect to major long-term issues and that X continue to reside with the mother. She otherwise recommended that X continue to spend professionally supervised time with the father (in addition to video calls if X wished to do so and if it could be done safely); but recommended that if the Court accepted the mother’s evidence with respect to her (and X’s) experiences of the father’s behaviour, there be no time spent between them.

The relevant legal principles

Part VII of the Act guides the process of decision-making in relation to the making of parenting orders. Section 60CA provides that when deciding whether to make a particular parenting order in relation to a child, the court must consider the best interests of the child as the paramount consideration. Section 60CC of the Act sets out how those best interests are to be determined, and includes a list of considerations to be taken into account. Each of those factors is considered below.

What arrangements would promote the safety (including safety from being subjected to, or exposed to, family violence, abuse, neglect, or other harm) of the child and each person who has care of the child

Meaning of safety

The word “safety” is not defined in the Act. It is defined in the Oxford English Dictionary as “the state of being protected from or guarded against hurt or injury; freedom from danger.”
In Oyama & Oyama,[12] Gill J held that “safety” in this context is a term that is wide in nature, and bounded only by its object, being to keep children, and those who care for them, from harm, and that it should not be taken to have a fixed content or standard. The paragraph requires the court to consider the risk of harm that accompanies the relationship, in whatever form it may take, and does not contemplate consideration of the benefits of a relationship where such is not safe.
In Denton & Denton (No 3),[13] Altobelli J expressed the view that “safety” is a state in which hazards or conditions leading to physical, psychological, or material harm are controlled in order to preserve the health and wellbeing of an individual. It is not the complete elimination of risk of harm, but rather making such order as affords the child the most optimal protection from harm.
In Melounis & Melounis (No 4),[14] Altobelli J held that to promote safety, for the purposes of subsection 60CC(2)(a), means “to advance the interests of, and move to a stronger or more prominent position, the safety (ie to be free from hurt, damage, harm or danger) of children and those who care for them” and that the statutory requirement to promote safety involves advancing a positive outcome, which would necessarily include protection from harm but would not be limited to this.
I respectfully adopt these definitions in determining the arrangements that would promote X’s safety and that of his primary carer.

Family violence

In considering this factor, it is necessary for the court to consider any history of family violence, abuse or neglect involving the child or a person caring for the child (whether or not the person had parental responsibility for the child) and any family violence order that applies or has applied to the child or a member of the child’s family.[15]
“Family violence” is defined in section 4AB of the Act to mean “violent, threatening or other behaviour by a person that coerces or controls a member of the person’s family (the family member), or causes the family member to be fearful.” It is not necessary that violent or threatening behaviour coerce or control or cause fear in order to be characterised as family violence.[16]
The concept of coercive and controlling violence has been described as “a course of conduct aimed at dominating and controlling another person,”[17] and as “the exercise of power, to restrain another or to cause another to act, by force, domination or command.”[18]
The concept of coercive control essentially captures one person’s domination over another. A person may subject another to coercive control without subjectively intending to do so. Intention is not a necessary element of coercive or controlling behaviour. A person who engages in such behaviour may be completely oblivious to the impact of their behaviour, but the behaviour may nevertheless coerce or control the other family member and fall within the definition of family violence. The focus is on the behaviour and its impact. A finding that there is coercive or controlling behaviour is an evaluative one, having regard to all of the circumstances and all of the evidence before the court.[19]
As Aldridge and Carew JJ held in Pickford & Pickford:[20]
The focus of the fact finding process is on the behaviour and the impact of the behaviour. It is the behaviour that coerces or controls. It requires action and reaction. A single act is unlikely to be coercive or controlling but it may be. Behaviour that coerces or controls may be innocuous, subtle, capable of different interpretations, complex, undermining, etc. Such behaviour may create impossible expectations for the other family member. It may be transactional and involve punishments for perceived failures. The impact of the behaviour can be insidious. A confident and happy family member may lose their confidence, their self-esteem, question their sanity, shun their family and friends, become depressed, irritable, inefficient, and unhappy. There may be times when they strike out either verbally or physically.
Section 60CG of the Act provides that in considering what order to make, the court must, to the extent that it is possible to do so consistently with the child's best interests being the paramount consideration, ensure that the order is consistent with any family violence order and does not expose a person to an unacceptable risk of family violence.
The mother alleged that the father had subjected her to physical, emotional, sexual and financial abuse and threatening and controlling behaviour during their relationship, including (but not limited to) the following:
(a) Isolating her from her family, including by preventing them from visiting her and preventing her from travelling to Adelaide to visit them. In this regard, the father admitted to Ms N that he had asked the maternal grandmother to leave the family home when she had come to assist the mother with the care of X shortly after his birth, as he perceived that this had been his role, despite acknowledging that the mother had experienced what he perceived as a “meltdown” upon an earlier departure of her mother. The mother’s evidence was of significantly abusive and aggressive conduct on behalf of the father in relation to this incident. He was evasive when her allegations were put to him in the witness box. The mother’s evidence was also that the father had refused to allow her to stay in Adelaide with family for the first couple of weeks following X’s birth, again during her recovery from a painful medical procedure she subsequently undertook in Adelaide, and again when X required medical treatment in Adelaide in mid-2021.

(b) Controlling her actions and movements. This was said to have included becoming upset and questioning her if she and X were not at home when he returned home from work during lunchbreaks; and requiring her to have her mobile telephone on aeroplane mode overnight and ringing her phone to check that she had done so (which conduct was admitted by the father).

(c) Yelling and shouting at her and calling her names, including “bitch,” “cunt” and “fucking pathetic.” The father admitted to having called the mother a “cunt,” a “psycho” and “fucking deluded.” The mother’s evidence was of this having occurred in X’s presence, including at times when the father was holding X. She alleged that the father had excused his behaviour by saying “I can’t hit you so that is all I can do” and had sought to blame her for “backing [him] into a corner.” The father admitted to this latter comment but attributed it to the mother being in a “frenzy,” banging on the door of the room he was in and screaming. In relation to some of the mother’s allegations of verbal abuse, the father was unable to say with any certainty whether or not he had engaged in the behaviour alleged.

(d) Making threats such as to cut off her hair while she slept.

(e) Making derogatory comments about her body and her sexual performance. This was admitted by the father.

(f) Pressuring her to engage in sexual activity when she did not wish to do so and engaging in sexual acts without her consent.

(g) Hitting her hard in the arm with a kayak paddle causing bruising, of which she adduced photographs.[21]

(h) Throwing items, including throwing a baby monitor after being asked to assist with the care of X.

The father indicated to Ms N that both he and the mother had been in the roles of “victim and oppressor” at times during their marriage. Implicit in this statement is an admission that he had played the role of oppressor. Similarly, in the witness box, the father asserted that both parties had “lashed out” at each other verbally.
The father put to Ms N that it was strange that “someone of below average intelligence” could psychologically abuse a highly intelligent medical professional. Ms N did not agree with that proposition and nor do I. Such a perspective does not align with contemporary understandings as to diversity of experiences of family violence.
The father also suggested to Ms N that he could not have committed financial abuse if he had not had access to bank accounts. The father did not establish that he had lacked access to bank accounts, but in any event, I do not accept this proposition. The evidence demonstrates that the father engaged in other conduct that was financially abusive including refusing to pay the mortgage despite remaining in the former matrimonial home while the mother was on parental leave and not in receipt of child support, and engaging in wholly inappropriate and counterproductive communications with her lawyers, causing her unnecessary expense.
The mother first reported her allegations as to family violence during the parties’ relationship to police in May 2022 (approximately nine months after separation), following an altercation between the parties at a handover. The father suggested that this was indicative of the allegations having been “opportunistic.” I do not agree. It is now well recognised that it is not at all uncommon for victims of family violence not to report their experiences immediately.[22] In Friseal & Friseal,[23] the Full Court of this Court (“the Full Court”) issued a warning about the problematic nature of fact finding in reliance upon an expectation that those who have experienced family violence will behave or present in a certain way.
The mother further alleged that the father had behaved inappropriately towards X prior to the parties’ separation, such as by becoming frustrated, forcibly turning X’s head and saying “drink, you little fuck” while trying to feed him; and making inappropriate comments to him, such as “mummy fucked up.”
I accept the mother’s allegations as to the family violence to which she was subjected during the relationship. Her evidence was coherent and detailed, and significantly, the father’s denials were unconvincing.
The evidence before the Court (including but not limited to documentary evidence) also supports a finding that the father also engaged in other threatening, abusive, harassing, antagonistic, dysregulated and otherwise concerning behaviour following the parties’ separation. That conduct included (but was not limited to) the following:
(a) Contacting X’s childcare centre on two occasions about the prospect of collecting him (despite there being no order or agreement permitting him to do so) and becoming angry upon being told he could not. The father informed the childcare centre of his opinion that they had “no legal right” to prevent him from collecting X and would be “breaking the law” if they tried to do so, before threatening to take the childcare worker’s children to “see if [she] liked it.” The father also emailed the centre referring to his “right” to his child.[24] In the witness box, the father unconvincingly claimed that his concern had been what would happen if the mother was sick and unable to collect X, despite the fact that the mother has several family members living in Adelaide and the father lives hundreds of kilometres from Adelaide.

(b) Posting a comment in the childcare app in February 2023 saying “[t]hanks for the update. My latest photos if [sic] [X] are over 4 months old, so much appreciated. Matthew 5:5 Those who own a sword and know how to use it – BUT KEEP IT SHEATHED – will inherit the world ☺.”[25] The father admitted having posted this comment. Approximately a week after he did so, X’s enrolment at the childcare centre was terminated. The father persistently demonstrated a lack of recognition of the serious and concerning nature of his conduct in this regard. In the witness box, when asked who he had meant could inherit the world, he facetiously replied that senior counsel for the mother could, before saying his intention in writing the message was “up to your interpretation” and for others to “better themselves.” Even with the benefit of hindsight, he was unable to accept that the comment might have been perceived as concerning or threatening. To Ms N, he asserted that there was “no factual evidence” that his behaviour had been the cause of the termination of X’s childcare enrolment. He alleged, implausibly, that X’s childcare enrolment had been terminated because the centre identified that he was being “alienated” from X, which “hurt them morally and ethically.” In the witness box, he refused to accept that the message had been the cause of the termination of X’s childcare enrolment.

(c) Making other inappropriate comments in the childcare app, referring to the mother having unilaterally relocated and unilaterally suspended time, and complaining of the “lengths some go to prevent our relationship.”[26] The father admitted to these allegations and confirmed that he “assumed” that the messages had really been directed at the mother, as an attempt to address what he (repeatedly) referred to as her “wilful ignorance.” As submitted on behalf of the ICL, the father’s communications with the childcare centre were not child-focused. They were attempts to control the narrative in a manner that invaded what should have been an emotionally safe space for X. Concerningly, the father was repeatedly warned about making inappropriate comments in the app after these messages, but persisted nonetheless.

(d) Having a person unknown to the mother take photographs of her and X in a park, and then sending one of the photographs to the mother approximately two months later in the context of a complaint about the pram the mother provided to him for use during his time with X not being as good as the one shown in the picture (in circumstances where the mother was at that time providing prams for use by the father both in Adelaide and in City K despite the father paying no child support). The father’s evidence about this incident was unsatisfactory. He admitted that he had received photographs of X and the mother from a third party and had subsequently sent a photograph to the mother. He asserted that the third party had been the owner of the motor inn where he stayed when in Adelaide to see X. He denied, and in fact laughed at, the proposition that he had instructed her to take the photographs, yet his version of events was highly improbable. He asserted that the woman had recognised X and taken the photograph without being requested to do so by him, and that he had been “shocked” to receive it, but denied that there had been any reason to report such odd behaviour to the mother or police and admitted that he had continued to stay at the motor inn after this incident. He also asserted that it was “not his fault” that the woman had not introduced herself to the mother. He denied having intended to create in the mother an understanding that he was following her movements despite not being in Adelaide. I do not accept that denial. Sending such a photograph could reasonably be expected to have made the mother believe that he was keeping her under surveillance and cause her to feel fearful or at least unsettled, even if that was not its only intended purpose. I note however, that even if the father’s evidence had been accepted, his actions in sending that photograph to the mother in the manner he did were entirely insightless.

(e) On the occasion of X’s third birthday, the father sent him a parcel. The outside of the parcel read “[f]rom loving father, victim of parental alienation." Inside the parcel was a gift bag with the words “live, laugh, love, bitch” printed on it. Inside a birthday card for X, the father had written:

Happy Birthday my beautiful son, another birthday/Christmas I have missed due to one persons [sic] personal mental issues. ALWAYS someone to blame. I love you always and can't wait to speak man to man about how evil some people can be. Have a great day and life (if we get to see each other in future). Love always DAD.[27]
The father admitted to having done this. Concerningly, this was done after the father had expressly acknowledged to the single expert psychiatrist, Dr Begg, his awareness that saying negative things about the mother would not be good for X. Under cross-examination, the father sought to justify this behaviour as having been partly a joke and partly an attempt to cause the mother to reflect on her behaviour and “realise [her] mistakes,” linking it to him not having been permitted to have a video call with X for his birthday and the mother’s original suspension of time between them. This was an admission by the father that he had conducted himself in the way he had to try and change the mother’s behaviour, which is indicative of coercive control. He also made the nonsensical suggestion that he had intended the card to be for himself rather than for X, and implausibly denied the suggestion that the reference to mental health issues had been a reference to the mother. In his closing submissions, the father suggested this incident had been “taken out of context” and suggested the mother should “bury the hatchet and move forward in life.” The family violence order was in force at the time the father sent these items containing messages which were plainly aimed at the mother.

(f) For Christmas in 2022, the father delivered or caused to be delivered a card written to X containing the words “we will be together in the future, despite your mother trying so desperately and selfishly to keep us apart. I will never forget who my son is and you should always know who daddy is,” together with a soccer ball with the words “integrity, principle, decency and justice” written on it.[28] This conduct was admitted by the father.

(g) Denigrating the mother during video calls with X, including saying things to X such as “your mummy stole you away,” “naughty mummy” and “a horrible person is keeping you away from me;” and telling the mother “fuck you bitch, fuck you cunt.” The father told Ms N that he had only said ten per cent of what the mother alleged in this regard. Even if that were accepted as accurate, it would be highly concerning.

(h) Denigrating the mother to third parties. This included:

(i) emails to her solicitors referring to her “extremely dry vagina” and “extremely saggy breasts” (being communications admitted by the father).[29]

(ii) comments made by the father to Children’s Contact Service (“CCS”) workers, including alleging that the mother was “grooming” X.

(iii) an email to the maternal grandparents saying “SURELY, you MUST be ashamed of your daughter. Move the goal posts to suit your cognitive bias. Disgusting people, promote priest paedophilia by looking tje [sic] other way because it's YOUR daughter. And YOUR daughter wouldn't do anything bad.”[30]

(iv) a message to the mother’s sister saying (inter alia) “when are you going to accept that your sister is a horrible, horrible person?????” The father gave evidence that his purpose in sending this message had been “people in general having accountability for their actions.”

(v) a message to the mother’s sister (which the father himself tendered) saying “...your sister plans to be ‘wilfully ignorant’ to prevent me seeing our son, due to an insatiable appetite for control...”[31]

(i) Denigrating and engaging in highly antagonistic communications directed towards the mother’s lawyers, such as by:

(i) Referring to her solicitor as a “non-fatal cancer”[32] (an allegation which was admitted by the father). In the witness box, when asked whether he thought this was a useful communication, the father responded that it “depends if the person receiving it is introspective.” He gave similar responses in response to questions about numerous other inappropriate communications he had made to the mother’s solicitors.

(ii) Asking her solicitor “[a]re you glad you get to aid in parental alienation of a loving father?” (an allegation which was admitted by the father).

(iii) Writing to the mother’s solicitor alleging that “she is under the guidance from an organization know [sic] to alienate loving, capable and wonderful co-parents;”[33]

(iv) Writing to the mother’s solicitors referring to “the willfully [sic] ignorant Jacqui Ion [being the mother’s solicitor], who struggles to resolve disputes and only escalates matters.”

(v) Writing to the mother’s lawyers saying “[w]e all know Jacqui Ion is a disgusting human.”[34] The father admitted to having sent this communication and continued to assert in the witness box that this was his perception.

(vi) Writing to the mother’s lawyers referring to “...the disgusting Jacqui Ion, whom has a close relationship with a pedophile [sic]...” and to Ms Ion’s “despicable behaviour.” [35] The father admitted to having sent this communication.

(vii) Repeatedly writing to the mother’s lawyers accusing them of being “wilfully ignorant.” The father admitted to having done so.

(viii) Emailing the mother’s solicitors referring to their “despicable organisation.”[36]

The father admitted to these and to many similar communications[37] but refused to acknowledge their inappropriateness, instead accusing senior counsel for the mother of “nitpicking.”

(j) Attempting to control and dictate the terms of the mother’s engagement of her lawyers by repeatedly demanding that solicitors of his choice take over the conduct of the mother’s file.[38] The father admitted to the mother’s allegations in this regard but refused to accept that his conduct had been inappropriate. To the contrary, he repeatedly complained that his demands had not been complied with.

(k) Taking a dictatorial tone and approach in communications to the mother’s lawyers, in which he set out his expectations as to her behaviour.[39]

(l) Making inappropriate statements to X at handovers, such as “this is the police station, this is where mummy wants me fucking locked up.” In response to this allegation, under cross-examination, the father simply denied the use of the swear word. He accepted that it was “possible” that he had said words to the effect of the balance of the sentence as alleged.

(m) Knowingly allowing her to feel distress and fear about the whereabouts and welfare of the family dog and failing to respond to her requests for information about her, other than to say he “no longer owned” her. In the witness box, the father was highly evasive when asked about the dog, repeatedly and implausibly claiming that he could not recall what had occurred and emphasising his view that the dog was his alone. Senior counsel for the mother submitted, and I accept, that his conduct with respect to the issue of the dog could be characterised as punishing the mother for the parties’ separation or her post-separation conduct.

(n) Sending the mother an email in September 2021 attaching an extract from a book referencing a person who had shot a child whose mother had also been shot, because “without its mother the child could not live any longer.”[40] The father gave evidence under cross-examination that this had been “about the cognitive process.” He denied having sent this to the mother to cause her to be frightened. I do not accept that denial. No other reaction in the mother could reasonably have been anticipated. I accept the submissions of senior counsel for the mother that this communication was designed to cause the mother to feel fear about what the father was capable of, and that the mother’s fear about the risk of him harming X was reasonably founded in such circumstances.

(o) Sending the mother a text message in October 2021 warning her “it’s going to end badly. Very badly. Just let me see my son.” The mother was facilitating time between the father and X at that time. The father admitted to sending this message.

(p) Telling the mother in October 2021 “if a dog is a stray it will get put down, you are putting our son on the level of a bloody animal.” The father admitted to sending this message.

(q) Shoving past her at handovers on more than one occasion.

(r) Denigrating her at handovers, such as by calling her a “fucking idiot” and a “fucking nutjob.”

(s) Refusing to hand X back to her at the conclusion of his time with him unless she met demands such as informing him which days X attended childcare.

(t) Unilaterally changing the times and arrangements for handovers by refusing to hand X back to the mother at the agreed times and places, including but not limited to insisting on keeping X for only one hour instead of the agreed period of five hours on one occasion, and keeping him overnight without consent on a further occasion, this occurring in March 2022 when X was less than a year old.

(u) Attempting to control the mother’s use of language, such as by demanding that she refer to his time with X as “custody,” which he deemed to be the “correct” terminology for her to use. This was admitted by the father.

(v) Persistently obstructively refusing to correspond directly with the mother with respect to making arrangements for him to spend time with X, demanding that she stop “harassing” him by attempting to do so, and accusing her of attempting to make him breach the family violence order in force against him, despite that order permitting such communications.[41]

(w) Telling her to “hurry up or I might have sex with you” when she attended the former matrimonial home to collect her belongings.

(x) Yelling at her in May 2022 that she was “going down a dangerous path” when she informed him that she would not facilitate additional time with X if he was going to make it difficult, before hitting her hand or mobile phone, causing the phone to fall to the ground and stepping on it when she commenced filming his behaviour, all of which occurred in X’s presence. The father’s version of this incident was that in the context of the mother becoming hysterical and attempting to “snatch” X, her phone had “become doislodged [sic] and had a cracked phone screen.” In the witness box, the father claimed to have remained calm throughout this incident and said he did not recall stepping on the mother’s phone “on purpose.” This altercation occurred in the context of an argument about the mother having cancelled an appointment for X to have an influenza vaccination as he had cold symptoms, which the father believed was a deliberate strategy to avoid him attending the appointment (which he was insisting on doing without the mother’s consent). It was following this incident that the mother reported the history of violence and abuse to police. The mother’s version of the events of this day was consistent with the level of anger still clearly felt by the father when describing them in the witness box over three years later.

(y) Telling X during a video call “don’t look at me like I’m a stranger, your cunt of a mother relocated on you” and “[X], your mother’s a fucking cunt.”

(z) Sending her a taunting text message (despite the existence of the family violence order) referring to her “saggy grandma tits” and claiming that “putting up with them was abuse against [him].”[42] This was one of a number of messages and other communications the mother alleged she had received from the father in contravention of the family violence order. The father sought to attribute blame to the mother for his conduct in this regard, suggesting it had occurred because he had been “so upset” and had “held onto some resentment” after the mother had made a police report against him.

(aa) Leaving a large canvas photograph from the parties’ wedding day outside the mother’s sister’s home. Despite asserting that this item had been left not at the mother’s sister’s home but “down the road hanging next to a garbage bin,” the father asserted facetiously that he had done this because he had thought maybe the mother wanted it because it was a lovely photograph of the [Region M landscape].

The contents of reports from the CCS dated 8 August 2024,[43] 24 November 2024[44] and 30 September 2025[45] support the mother’s evidence. The authors of those reports were not cross-examined and the accuracy of the reports was therefore not challenged. The reports reveal conduct on behalf of the father that was concerning and lacking in child focus. That conduct includes:
(a) Making complaints about being required to follow the child-focused policies of the CCS, and failing to adhere to those policies. This included complaining (during his time with X) that it was “demoralising that a father could not even take photos with his son.” He also complained about workers (appropriately) intervening when he denigrated the mother in X’s presence because he “did not like to be bossed around.”

(b) On several occasions, the father’s complaints about the workers implementing the CCS policies escalated into altercations. For example:

(i) when reminded about the policy that phones were not to be used during observations, the CCS report records that the father’s face turned red, he began shouting “this is fucking ridiculous” and continued shouting, using swear words, as he spoke about the court system, the CCS and the CCS workers being “ridiculous.” In the witness box, the father dismissed the description of his face turning red as being “a generic thing to say.”

(ii) At a visit in July 2025, the father tried to insist on a change to the centre’s usual process whereby the worker checked in with X in the playroom before the father was brought in, instead wanting to be the one enter the playroom first. He told the CCS staff that this was to “balance the power in the room” and asserted that he was the only one who knew what was best for X. When this was refused, he pointed toward the exit, told the supervisor to put her ego outside, and told her that she did not need to show her authority about being the boss. He then became more heightened and said the supervisor was not listening to him, and was being egotistical and undermining him, despite him knowing what was best for X. The supervisor left the area, and approximately two minutes later, heard a loud crashing sound coming from where she had left the father. She returned and observed that one of the tables was on an angle, and bottles and tumblers that had been on it were on the floor. The father said that he was in a foul mood because of the supervisor, whom he said was upsetting him and was being “egotistical.” He told her to “fix her mood.” In the witness box, the father continued to complain about the imbalance of power between himself and the supervisor. He was unable to accept that this incident was an example of him being unable to control his behaviour when he did not get his own way and instead characterised it as “an example of frustration when [he is] powerless.” He also asserted that the supervisor “should not be imposing rules” and complained about her “attitude” and her failure to reflect on X’s exposure to his mood when deciding how to conduct herself. He implausibly claimed to have knocked the table “mistakenly.”

When interviewed by Ms N, the father suggested that it was the CCS staff who should have handled the situation better on these occasions.

(c) Questioning X about whether he remembered him and whether he wanted more time with him, including by asking “who am I, buddy, who am I?” “will you remember Daddy?” “do you miss Daddy?” and “don’t you want to see Daddy more?” When asked to accept that this last question had been inappropriate, the father evaded the question and instead asserted that it had been “premature” and that if he asked the question now, X’s answer would be “yes.” The father also asked X whether he was ready for him to be a “read dad instead of a play dad” and have a “real father/son relationship,” and asked him, “are you [X Praset] or [X Janek]?” Of this last question, the father was asked whether it might have caused X some confusion and responded, “it could have caused him some confusion and perhaps some clarity.”

(d) Questioning X when he said, “Mummy’s my best friend,” trying to persuade him that he, the father, was in fact his best friend; and asking him, “what about Dad, do you like Dad” when he said, “Mum is my favourite.” The father also asked X, “what does Mum say about Dad?”

(e) Telling X that his coordination had gone backwards and saying, “you need a daddy in your life.”

(f) Denigrating the mother to X, for example:

(i) Telling him, “your mummy’s a bit naughty” and saying “yes” when X responded “no.” When the worker appropriately intervened and offered X reassurance that his mother was not “naughty,” the father argued with the worker, saying “is that your opinion? In my opinion she is.” When a supervisor was called in to and asked the father to step out discuss safe talk, he said “[n]o actually my son and I are having a good time. and we don’t need to have the judgment” and suggested that the worker should not have intervened. When it was put to him that he had upset X with these comments, he agreed he may have upset him “slightly” but not “too much” and “we were engaging in nice safe play, it wasn’t required that we have a break.” Ms N gave evidence that being told that his mother was “naughty” would have been damaging to X, who relies on his mother for every aspect of his physical and emotional care and safety.

(ii) Saying to X “Mummy didn't let me video call you on your birthday, did she?”

(iii) Telling him, “Daddy loves you more than Mummy.” In the witness box, the father attempted to excuse this remark by explaining that he [the father] was “still hurt from the time apart.”

(g) Attempting to justify his derogatory comments about the mother by saying he “did not want to lie to [X].”

(h) Asking X to do things to make him happy and refusing to respect X’s wishes, such as by posing for a photograph, and persisting when X showed no interest and even when he expressly refused.

(i) Making offers to X that he could not fulfil, such as offering to take him to kindergarten and to a sports game when the orders permitted only professionally supervised time.

(j) Burdening X with his own feelings, such as by telling him “Dad's gonna be sad for a little bit." In response to this, X said, "why are you gonna be a little bit sad?" and the father responded "because I won't see you for a little bit. I want to see you more.” Shortly afterwards, X said “Dad, I will be a little bit sad as well,” to which the father responded “you will? Because you won't see me?” Upon returning to his mother, X said “my dad will be a little bit sad.” X appeared to remember this at the next visit, which was approximately a month later, when he asked his father “will you be sad?" to which the father responded “yes, I will be sad.” The father was dismissive about his conduct in this regard, saying that he had told X of his sadness because “it's a matter of fact.” Ms N agreed under cross-examination that this would have been a lot for a child of X’s age to deal with, and that at worst it was coercive because it invited X to adopt the proposition of sadness in order to satisfy the father’s emotional needs.

It was submitted on behalf of the mother, and I accept, that these reports demonstrated that even in a supervised context, the father is not capable of consistently behaving in a child-focused manner. The ICL likewise submitted that these reports reflect worrying interactions between the father and X in which the father was unwilling or unable to interpret X’s verbal and non-verbal cues or remain child-focused and the intervention of CCS staff was required.
The mother’s allegations with respect to the father’s post-separation conduct were also supported by other independent evidence before the court. An example of this was a South Australia Police Custom Occurrence Report[46] which recorded that in May 2023, police contacted the father, who “began ranting about his ex-partner and the police for approximately 5 minutes straight” and asserted that it was a “twisted situation” with people using “the law as a weapon,” that “for all he knows his son could be dead” and that if the police had just done their job and conducted a 111 [welfare check] on his son, he wouldn't have tripped up to check on him.” The record suggests that the police formed concerns about the father’s mental health and encouraged him to contact his general practitioner and the telephone counselling service MensLine. On this occasion, the father unsuccessfully tried to persuade police to obtain a family violence order on his behalf against the mother.
I find that the father has engaged in an ongoing pattern of behaviour by which he has subjected the mother to family violence, including coercion, control and verbal and emotional abuse, following their separation and during the course of these proceedings. I am also satisfied that he has subjected and exposed X to inappropriate and harmful conduct which has jeopardised X’s emotional safety.
It is an agreed fact that the father has been charged with and convicted of three breaches of the family violence order (one of which was a representative count), including breaches involving communications sent to the mother and a breach involving driving outside a location where she was staying (which incident was captured on security camera footage).[47] He deposed in his trial affidavit to facing a charge for “another breach” of the family violence order. He minimised this conduct, however, claiming that his breaches were “benign” and in some cases accidental, and complained about the mother’s “unnecessary reporting to police.”
The father gave somewhat confusing oral evidence, the effect of which appeared to be that by the time of the trial he had been convicted of the charge referred to in his affidavit and was facing further charges for breaching the family violence order by contacting the mother’s sister and mother to request video calls with X. He said that he had not realised that he was not permitted to make such arrangements via third parties. What he did not disclose or accept responsibility for was that the voicemail messages he left were abusive and denigrated the mother. For example:
(a) to the mother’s sister, the father said:
she's trying to provoke a reaction out of me so she can use it against me in her case and that's her game and she's going to do anything she can do to, you know, beat me and to prove that I'm crazy or, you know, trying, you know, but um, yeah, it's pretty disgusting. I hope that yourself and [Mr O] [the mother’s sister’s husband] could, um, yeah, see what's going on there and, you know, sort of have a few words to [Ms Praset] and, you know, wake her up a bit because it's pretty disgusting.[48]
(b) to the maternal grandmother, the father said: “could you stop being wilfully ignorant? Actually like, you know, tell your daughter, you know, start being a decent human being for once”[49]
The mother alleged that the father lacked insight into the concerning nature of his conduct and its consequences, and that he tended to blame others for the consequences of his actions. This was reflected throughout his interview with Ms N and throughout his evidence, during which he repeatedly attributed blame for his circumstances to the mother and to others including the Court and the CCS, and during which he repeatedly failed to take responsibility for his own conduct or acknowledge its consequences. Examples of this (in addition to those mentioned elsewhere in these reasons) included:
(a) The father asserted that conflict between the parties had been caused by the mother’s “need for control” and his “subsequent overreaction” and “strong principles.”

(b) In the witness box, although he said that he accepted responsibility for his part in the current circumstances, he did not in fact accept such responsibility or demonstrate any real understanding of this issue. Rather, he continually asserted that the mother was to blame.

(c) The father repeatedly attempted to justify extremely poor conduct on his part by saying that it had occurred “out of frustration” but paradoxically refused to accept that he was unable to control his own behaviour when frustrated or angry. Whilst he admitted a tendency to overreact, he refused to accept that this was indicative of loss of control over his behaviour, yet also denied that he was in control of his behaviour at such times. Despite being confronted with numerous examples of inappropriate and uncontrolled behaviour on his part, he persistently refused to acknowledge limitations to his inability to control himself, despite the only alternative interpretation being that he consciously and deliberately engaged in poor behaviour.

(d) The father alleged that the mother had obtained the family violence order against him “to gain an advantage in litigation” despite the fact that this occurred well prior to the commencement of the proceedings.

(e) The father sought to minimise his serious conduct in the form of breaches of the family violence order as not “violent or threatening” but rather “out of frustration and being powerless,” blaming his behaviour on the mother’s “parental alienation.”

(f) The father repeatedly recounted incidents of poor behaviour on his behalf without demonstrating any understanding of its problematic nature. One example of this is his own evidence of having sent a text message to the mother’s sister accusing the mother of being “wilfully ignorant” and having an “insatiable appetite for control.”

(g) The father sought to excuse having called the mother a “bitch” as “reactive abuse,” which he attributed to an unparticularised allegation of “coercive control” on the part of the mother.

(h) The father referred to his communications in the childcare app (as described earlier) as “a couple of benign messages,” significantly minimising the serious and concerning nature of those communications, and persistently refused to accept responsibility for his conduct having deprived X of his ongoing enrolment at that centre. To Dr Begg, the father sought to explain his conduct with respect to the childcare centre as being because he felt “powerless” but admitted that it was also a “stab at” the mother. However, he sought to resile from this acknowledgment in the witness box.

(i) The father told Ms N that he did not think he was given enough credit for “walking away” rather than engaging in conflict, despite the extensive evidence of reactive and conflictual conduct on his part. Similarly, despite that evidence, he refused to accept under cross-examination that he had trouble regulating his emotions, insisting that he walks away from conflict.

(j) As already, mentioned, the father sought to attribute his conduct with respect to the card and gift sent to X for his third birthday to the mother’s conduct in suspending time and not permitting a video call.

(k) In the witness box, the father repeatedly followed admissions of poor behaviour on his part with allegations against the mother. For example, having admitted to calling her a “cunt,” he asserted that this had been because he felt “seriously threatened” and was “in desperate fear for [his] safety.” When it was put to him that he had also called her that following separation, he sought to excuse this by saying it had been in response to the mother failing to facilitate video calls between himself and X. He sought to explain a threat to kick down a door by saying the mother had been being “violent and immature.” He also described abusive behaviour on his part as “reactive abuse.”

(l) In the witness box and in his closing submissions, he was at pains to emphasise that he had not mentioned his view about the mother having “saggy grandma tits” until after she had had them “fixed,” apparently believing that this rendered the conduct less abusive or concerning. He refused to accept that it was likely that his conduct in this regard had caused the mother distress. He was similarly either unwilling or unable to accept that sending similar communications to the mother’s solicitors was likely to cause her embarrassment and distress.

(m) Under cross-examination, the father asserted that the CCS workers had intervened during his time with X not because he had behaved inappropriately, but because X was “such a lovely boy” and suggested that the workers needed to “have discipline, sit there and write the report, not engage with us.”

(n) When asked by counsel for the ICL whether he took any responsibility for the fact that he had not spent unsupervised time with X since 2022, the father was not meaningfully able to do so.

(o) Ms N opined that the father had not demonstrated any insight into the likely impact on the mother of his derogatory comments about her body and sexual performance. She gave evidence that his willingness to express these sentiments to third parties such as her solicitors suggested either that he was unable to interact in a manner that was appropriate or that he was engaging in such conduct intentionally to cause the mother emotional harm and humiliation. I accept the submission of counsel for the ICL that this conduct in deliberately causing harm and humiliation to the mother was coercive in nature, being imposed as a punishment for the mother not having acted in the way the father had wanted her to.

(p) Ms N further opined that the father’s interactions with the CCS staff, as recorded in their reports, indicated that he was incapable of responding to external input in which his behaviour was called into question.

I accept the submission of counsel for the ICL that throughout his oral evidence, the father consistently minimised the impact of his behaviour on X and on the mother and showed little insight into and took minimal responsibility for, the consequences of his actions.
Ms N observed that the father appeared to struggle to reflect upon his own role in situations which had arisen and to have difficulty reflecting upon his own behaviour despite repeatedly claiming to be willing to do so. Examples of this included the following:
(a) The father dismissed the mother’s concerns as “just that, concerns” and described her as “paranoid.”

(b) After being asked twice whether he had ever done something to contribute to the mother being worried about him denigrating her to X, he claimed to have “acted inappropriately to her controlling behaviour” and appeared to Ms N to struggle to tolerate further discussion about his own conduct. In the witness box, Ms N said that she had felt compelled to moderate the manner in which she interacted with the father and discontinue the line of questioning about his conduct in order to enable him to regulate his emotions.

(c) When asked about the card he had sent for X’s third birthday, he attributed blame to the mother, saying “I’m reactive because his mum is a horrible, controlling person.” When asked what he thought that would be like for X to have such a card read to him, the father did not reflect on that important question and instead gave the self-focused response that there was a family violence order in place “so if I addressed it to [the mother], I would be in court again with another charge.”

(d) The father sought to excuse having denigrated the mother to X in the presence of the CCS staff as “a one off, a poor reaction one time” arising from not being good at “playing the game” and claimed that this would not have occurred had he not been “under [a] microscope” in the context of supervision. The evidence clearly establishes that the father’s denigration of the mother to X was by no means “a one off” but was rather an established pattern of behaviour on his part.

Ms N reported although the father had said that he took responsibility for his actions, he had not meaningfully conveyed that he had insight into his behaviour or willingness to make and sustain changes. Under cross-examination, she agreed that his expression of willingness to take responsibility had been a sentence without substance, noting that when asked for an example of when he had taken responsibility, the father had responded with an anecdote in which he attributed blame to the mother.
Ms N further observed that the father did not present as seeking to make changes to his personal functioning, and nor did he convey meaningful insight into or a genuine belief as to there being changes which he needed to make to improve his relationship with X. I formed the same view of the father’s assertions made in the witness box to the effect that he accepted responsibility for his behaviour. I accept the submission made on behalf of the mother that such assertions were made glibly and lacked genuine reflection.
The father undertook the Circle of Security parenting program.[50] He also gave evidence of having engaged a life coach, although there was no evidence before the Court as to the qualifications of the life coach or the nature of the work undertaken by the life coach with the father. The father submitted, and I accept, particularly in relation to the Circle of Security course, that these are positive steps taken by him and indicators of a willingness to make changes. However, the father’s ongoing conduct and presentation in the witness box did not reflect that having undertaken these processes had led to any meaningful development in his level of insight into his conduct and its impact. The father’s strong resistance to and inability to tolerate criticisms of his behaviour (as reflected in the CCS reports and in Ms N’s evidence) does not bode well in terms of the likelihood of his commitment to and success with any therapeutic engagement directed to addressing his conduct and bringing out meaningful change.
In his closing submissions, the father made a number of assertions to the effect that he accepted responsibility for his behaviour and was committed to change. However, these statements were undermined by the tenor of his evidence. They were also inconsistent with other aspects of his closing submissions, including the expression of a hope that allegations of family violence made by each of the parties would be put “at the same levels,” ongoing allegations that it was the mother who had engaged in coercive and controlling behaviour towards him, an assertion that the mother had suspended time “illegitimately,” and significant minimisation of his conduct towards the mother, which he described as “conflict” and his conduct towards X, which he sought to characterise as “unwitting.” He also described himself as “innocent.”
The father submitted that his reactiveness was a problem with “adult relationships,” not “child relationships,” meaning that X would not cause him to be reactive and would therefore be safe in his care. The difficulty with this submission is that the evidence reveals numerous examples of the father already having exposed X to unsafe behaviours.
The father’s lack of insight into the serious and concerning nature of his behaviour leaves no basis for any confidence that he will take any meaningful steps to address it or change his behaviour in future.
The father is, however, to be given credit for abandoning his application for joint decision-making with respect to major long-term issues. It was clear that this was done reluctantly and was not what the father wanted, and as such, it was a child-focused decision.
The father alleged that the mother had subjected him to emotional and financial abuse, including raising her voice at him (including in X’s presence), biting him, locking him out of the house and sticking her finger up at him, and throwing objects at him. He reported to Ms N (and repeated numerous times during the trial) that there had been a time when he had had to lock himself in a room while the mother had been banging on the door and screaming at him. To Ms N, he also described the mother as “very controlling.”
The mother admitted having bitten the father on one occasion, in the context of him having grabbed her from behind and forcefully restrained her. In this regard, the father admitted to having physically restrained her, which he attributed to her being in an “uncontrollable state.” The mother also admitted having locked a door on him when he had been acting aggressively towards her and she felt unsafe, and to having thrown parts of a breast pump at him in the context of him denigrating her while she was pumping breast milk.
I do not accept that the mother has subjected the father to family violence, other than reactively, in response to his treatment of her. I do not consider that any such conduct on the part of the mother renders her a risk to X’s safety.

Father’s mental health

The suspension of the father’s time with X in September 2022 occurred following the mother being informed by an accountant named Mr P, who had been engaged by the father to assist with the parties’ financial negotiations, that he held concerns about the father’s mental health. The mother reported that Mr P had told her that the father had displayed concerning behaviour and appeared “fixated on dominating” her. Mr P had also expressed concern about the “extreme” risk that the father could harm himself and X.
The mother did not suggest that Mr P had the qualifications to diagnose mental health conditions, nor that she had believed that he did. Her evidence was that she had already held concerns about the father’s mental health, and that these concerns were exacerbated both by Mr P’s comments and by her discussions with a friend of the father, who had expressed concern about the state of the father’s health and had informed her that the father had been talking about suicide. The mother’s reliance not only on Mr P’s statements but on the manner in which the father interacted with her in making the decision to suspend X’s time with him was made clear in contemporaneous correspondence.[51]
The father himself had also sent the mother a message (albeit a year earlier, in September 2021) expressing concerns about his own mental health,[52] and he sent another shortly after the time was suspended making reference to his mental health deteriorating (a state of affairs responsibility for which he attributed to the mother).[53] A medical record dated December 2023 also records that the father had experienced a “gradual mood deterioration since separation.”[54] The mother formed the view that it was no longer safe to facilitate time between X and the father. Her solicitor informed the father that she would not facilitate time pending a psychiatric assessment.
The father presented as fixated on Mr P’s role in the cessation of X’s unsupervised time with him, repeatedly asserting that Mr P was the reason he was not seeing X. He told Ms N that the issues raised with respect to his mental health were “unsubstantiated slanderous comments” and alleged that Mr P had lied to the mother about his alleged concerns due to a disagreement with the father over an unpaid invoice. He was highly critical of the mother for having ceased the time arrangement under what he characterised as “illegitimate circumstances.”
Shortly after the parties’ interactions with Mr P, it became known to them that he had been charged with criminal offences. The father also repeatedly referred to a lawsuit allegedly taken by Mr P against Q Business which was said to have led to the business being shut down, the relevance of which was unclear, but which he claimed “shows the kind of person [Mr P] is.” The father accepted that the mother had not been aware of these asserted issues with respect to Mr P’s character until after she had made the decision to suspend X’s time with him.
Despite acknowledging to Ms N that he understood why the mother had believed what she had been told about his mental health, the father was intensely focused on Mr P’s alleged criminal and other activities and was highly critical of the mother for having acted on the advice of a person he described as being of “questionable character.” He deposed to holding an opinion that the mother had exhibited “cognitive bias” and that she and her lawyer had been “wilfully ignorant” regarding Mr P. The father also returned to this issue numerous times during his oral evidence.
The father’s behaviour in relation to this issue included sending the mother articles about Mr P with emails saying:
I trust that in seeing these photos, you will realise your wrongdoing in cooperating with a despicable white collar criminal/pedophile [sic] to prevent me seeing our son. That you will admit fault and facilitate me being able to see [X] in an unsupervised capacity moving forward. I have printed and filed these in my folder for [X] to read when he is at a sufficient age
and
I kindly advise that when future [X] reads this email he may think you are an evil, horrible person, who is preventing me from seeing him for 16 months and counting due to a pedophile [sic] named [Mr P]. That his mother has collaborated with the lowest of the low, a disgusting filth to keep me away from you. She has used the SAPOL as an asset and used the #metoo era in her favour by utilizing the law a [sic] weapon against me.[55]
The father was charged and convicted of a breach of the family violence order for sending these emails. Rather than acknowledging the seriousness of his conduct, the father complained in the witness box that the mother was “incapable of restraining herself from reporting [him] to police,” asserting that this was “an issue surrounding the family law order which didn’t need to be in the Magistrates Court.”
Whilst it is understandable that the father felt aggrieved by the cessation of X’s time with him in September 2022, and I accept the father’s submission that the events of that month represented a turning point in the post-separation developments in this matter, his ongoing fixation on the wrongdoings and “character” of Mr P (which are peripheral issues) rather than on the central issue of his own conduct and its impact on the mother and X, indicated a lack of insight or reflective capacity. As submitted by counsel for the ICL, by the time of the commencement of the proceedings, and particularly by the time of the trial, the matter had well and truly moved on from the comments of Mr P, but it was this, and not the multitude of issues directly relevant to X and his best interests, on which the father remained focused.
A psychiatric assessment of the father prepared by Dr Jules Begg dated in August 2023 was tendered by the father.[56] Dr Begg opined that there was no obvious psychiatric disorder diagnosis, and he did not diagnose a personality disorder. He indicated that there was no need for specific treatment. Dr Begg further observed that the father’s presentation was not consistent with someone who wanted to harm their child. Dr Begg considered it unlikely that he would perpetrate future violence. Dr Begg did not see a psychiatric or personality reason that the father should not have unsupervised time with X.
It was submitted on behalf of the mother that no weight could be given to Dr Begg’s opinion because he had not had available to him the father’s responding material. Dr Begg was not required for cross-examination, and the extent (if any) to which his opinion may have changed as a result of further information becoming available to him is therefore not known.
Dr Begg’s opinion that the father was unlikely to perpetrate future violence turned out not to be accurate, as reflected in the clear evidence of extensive conduct meeting the definition of family violence engaged in by the father following Dr Begg’s report.
The evidence before the Court does not enable a definitive finding as to whether the father poses a risk to X by reason of his mental health.

Mother’s mental health

The father made some statements suggesting that he considered that the mother suffered from mental ill health. For example, he alleged (in response to an allegation of sexual penetration of the mother without her consent) that the mother “appears to suffer from delusions.” He also made a number of other allegations of behaviour he described as “uncontrollable.” The contents of the birthday card sent to X also suggest an allegation relating to the mother’s mental health.
