A psychiatric assessment of the mother prepared by Dr Begg did not suggest that the mother’s mental health posed a risk to X.[57] Notably, the father’s proposal that X remain in the primary care of the mother suggests that he does not consider that X is at risk in her care. I am not satisfied that the mother poses a risk to X, by reason of mental health or otherwise.

Any views expressed by the child

As a result of X’s age, there is limited information before the court as to his views, other than that he indicated to Ms N that he likes to play with both his parents. Given his age, his views can be given limited weight.

The developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the child

Ms N opined that as a young child, X is developmentally vulnerable, and that ongoing exposure to situations which he finds stressful and which are beyond his emotional and cognitive capacities to manage, may compromise his wellbeing.
The mother reported to Ms N that X is meeting his developmental milestones and is generally in good health. This accorded with Ms N’s own observations. She opined that this would be attributable to the mother’s appropriate parenting of him.
The father told Ms N that X’s gross motor development was “worse” than it had been two years prior when he had been more involved in his life. He attributed this to what he imagined was the mother’s lack of time to “go out and kick a ball with [X]” as a result of her work commitments. He also indicated on a checklist completed for Ms N that he was worried about X’s language development because the mother is introverted and works long hours.
I prefer Ms N’s evidence, based on her expertise and experience, as to X’s developmental progress and am not satisfied that there is any cause for concern in this regard.

The capacity of each person who has or is proposed to have parental responsibility for the child to provide for the child's developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs

Mother’s parenting capacity and attunement

Ms N observed positive, warm and supportive interactions between X and his mother. She reported that observations of X with the mother suggested that he shares a secure relationship with her, and he presents as having his developmental needs met in her care. The mother impressed to Ms N as consistently child-focused throughout the assessment.
The father asserted by way of his case outline that the mother had proven that she was unable to act in X’s best interests. That allegation was not supported by the preponderance of the evidence before the court. It was also at odds with the father’s position that X should remain in the mother’s primary care.
The father alleged that the mother was unable to meet X’s physical and mental needs while caring for a newborn baby. This assertion was unsupported by any evidence and was also inconsistent with the father’s own proposal. When cross-examined by the father, Ms N asserted, and I accept, that parents are often able to manage the competing needs of more than one child, including when caring for newborns. The birth of a sibling is not evidence of the mother’s ability to meet X’s needs being compromised.
In his closing submissions, the father confirmed that he did not suggest that the mother had been abusive or negligent with respect to X.

Father’s parenting capacity and attunement

It is apparent from the evidence before the Court, including but not limited to the evidence of observations of the father interacting with X (the family report and the CCS reports) that the father loves X, genuinely wishes to be actively involved in his life, and experiences his lack of opportunity to engage with him as a source of loss and grief. However, the evidence also reveals cause for concern with respect to the father’s level of attunement to X and capacity to meet his emotional needs.
The father was observed to frequently seek physical contact with X, but not to consistently respond to X’s wishes when they did not align with his own. For example, he was observed to continue to tickle X after X said “no,” resulting in X moving away from the father on several occasions during the observation. Ms N observed that both X’s verbal and non-verbal cues in this regard indicated that he wanted the father to stop, but the father did not do so. In the witness box, she described this as X not receiving an attuned response.
Ms N observed the father to incorporate themes into his play with X which she did not consider child-focused, such as telling X that “sometimes the police are baddies” and saying “I haven't seen my daddy, my daddy's innocent.” In a later interview, the father made a statement which was interpreted by Ms N as acknowledging the inappropriateness of this latter statement. In the witness box, Ms N explained that the father’s comments regarding police could have affected X’s understanding of the world as a safe place by providing a perspective which he was too young to understand and did not have the cognitive capacity to manage, which could lead to him becoming distrustful.
Ms N reported that X was also observed to engage in behaviours which suggested he struggled with the demands placed upon him when with his father. In particular, she opined that X’s presentation following the observation with the father (which included dysregulation followed by a flat affect) gave rise to concern about the impact on X of his interactions with the father.
Ms N also reported that the father may be looking to X to validate his own emotional needs and may struggle to see X’s needs as separate from his own.
The father’s proposal that X shift immediately from the current situation of no time following very limited supervised time to a regime involving extended overnight time was also indicative of a lack of sensitivity to X’s developmental and emotional needs.
The CCS reports also record some interactions between X and the father which are suggestive of a lack of attunement on the father’s part to X’s needs. These include failing either to recognise or respect verbal and non-verbal cues from X, particularly indications that X did not want to be touched. This took the form of continuing to put his arms around X when X moved away from his embrace, repeatedly asking X for hugs and ignoring X’s reluctance and repeatedly (on multiple separate occasions) continuing to tickle X, insisting that X liked it, after X told him to stop. When the CCS worker advised him to take note of when X says no to tickles and hugs, the father said “I know that. I am not stupid.”
In the witness box, the father insisted that on such occasions, X had actually wanted him to continue tickling or hugging him, but had simply not been able articulate himself properly, but that the father could tell. He also asserted that repeatedly questioning X about why he could not tickle him was reading his cues. Ms N agreed under cross-examination that these answers gave rise to concern about the extent to which the father had reflected on the contents of her report. She opined that if the father continued to impose his own views as to X’s desires over X’s express wishes, this would give rise to concern about X compromising his own needs, and to concern about how the father may respond if X asserted his needs and they did not align with those of the father.
It should be noted that there were occasions upon which it is apparent that the father did read X’s cues and appropriately assess his needs. For example, he did stop tickling him on several occasions when X said he did not want to be tickled. On one occasion, he recognised from X repeatedly looking at the door that he wished to play outside, and on another, he noticed that X was looking towards juice and correctly deduced that he wanted some. However, taken as a whole, the evidence of the observations of the father and X together supports a finding that the father does not consistently understand or respond to X’s verbal and non-verbal cues.

Mother’s level of support of relationship between father and child

In the material filed by him, to Ms N, and repeatedly in the witness box, the father accused the mother of “parental alienation.” During his oral evidence, it emerged that the father did not in fact allege that the mother had spoken poorly of him to X or attempted to influence X’s views of him, but rather, his complaint was that the mother was unwilling to support or facilitate X’s relationship with him. The father’s case was that this reflected poorly on the mother’s ability to meet X’s emotional needs.
The father alleged that the mother had failed to keep him informed about X’s medical treatment and development and had failed to provide him with photographs or information about X’s general wellbeing. The mother demonstrated[58] that she had provided the father with monthly updates about X’s health and development and had often provided photographs, from November 2021 until 11 January 2023. The monthly updates were appropriate, detailed and child-focused.
The mother’s provision of regular updates to the father ceased when the father demanded that she stop, sending her a message saying (inter alia) “[u]nless it is to facilitate seeing [our] son or notifying me of his whereabouts, there is no need to inform me useless information. At best this is condescending and degrading. Probably closer to physcological (sic) bullying and harassment.” This was admitted by the father. In such circumstances, the father’s complaint is entirely unwarranted and simply represents an example of a broader tendency on his part to blame the mother for the consequences of his own behaviour. Furthermore, the father’s characterisation of the mother’s entirely appropriate behaviour as “bullying and harassment” provides a lens through which the father’s allegations against the mother must be viewed.
The mother gave evidence of significant lengths she had gone to following the parties’ separation to facilitate X having a relationship with his father. These included offering to return to live in the former matrimonial home in City K (which the father refused to vacate), travelling to City K with X two weekends out of five (at her own expense despite not receiving any child support and despite being on parental leave for approximately the first six months, and in the face of very difficult, obstructive behaviour towards her on the part of the father), purchasing a car to use when she travelled to City K to facilitate time, facilitating time in Adelaide, authorising his access to the childcare centre app and involving him in significant events such as X’s first haircut.
The CCS reports and the family report demonstrate that X consistently transitioned easily from each of his mother and grandmother to his father. Ms N gave evidence that this could suggest that the mother had been doing what she could to support their relationship. This was consistent with the mother’s evidence that she prepared X to enjoy spending time with the father. The mother’s willingness and ability to do so in the face of the father’s extremely difficult conduct towards her is a testament to her child focus.
Ms N reported the mother having presented as being supporting of X having a relationship with the father if it was safe to do so.
I am not satisfied that the mother has demonstrated an unreasonable unwillingness or inability to support X having a relationship with his father. To the contrary, the evidence before the Court supports a finding that the mother has gone to significant lengths to support their relationship, but that she has also acted protectively in imposing limitations on that relationship when she has considered that X’s safety required her to do so.

Father’s level of commitment to engagement with child

The mother alleged that the father had exhibited an intermittent commitment to fostering his own relationship with X, including inconsistent commitment to pursuing and then making himself available for time and communication, with the result that there had frequently been long gaps between their interactions, which had inhibited the development of a bond between them.
As already mentioned, the father refused a request made by the mother that he vacate the former matrimonial home to enable her to return to City K and live there with X following the parties’ separation. The mother’s evidence was that she had no family or friends in City K with whom she could live at that time. She was also on parental leave and not in paid employment. The father proposed that the mother and X reside in the upstairs portion of the house while he continue to reside downstairs. In light of his conduct towards the mother (as discussed throughout these reasons), that was not a realistic proposal. The consequence of the father’s position in this regard was that the mother was left with no realistic alternative but to remain with her family in Adelaide. She lived with her parents until she returned to work. As submitted by counsel for the ICL, the father’s refusal to vacate the former matrimonial home when requested to do so by the mother had a direct (and, I would add, entirely foreseeable) impact on the manner in which X’s relationship with his father was able to develop.
On 10 August 2022, the father sent the mother a text message saying “[a]m I also seriously considering walking away from him because of what you are doing to me? Well...”[59] He gave evidence that “as per my text message” there were times he had considered walking away. He blamed this on the mother’s actions. Her actions immediately prior to that text message had merely been to ask him to confirm that he intended to have time with X during the upcoming weekend and, in turn, to confirm that she would be bringing X to City K for that purpose.
Similarly, on 14 October 2022, the father sent a message to the mother saying:
...I am not going to fight this. It's up to YOU to decide if you want [X] to grow up without a father. The ball is in YOUR court because I am getting myself ready to walk away... Anyway, don't blame me from walking away from [X], it is YOU who has the ability to admit mistakes have been made and to allow a father/son relationship...[60]
After being asked repeatedly whether this was a reflection that he had been intending to walk away, the father gave the non-responsive answer that it was “a reflection of the zeitgeist.”
The mother deposed to several occasions during the period when X was spending time with the father pursuant to the agreement between them when he did respond to her requests for confirmation as to whether he would be attending and did not respond to offers for additional time or changes to the arrangements to accommodate special occasions. On several such occasions, he accused her of harassing him and directed her to stop. The father also failed to attend for X’s scheduled time with him in Adelaide in August 2022.
When his time was suspended pending a psychiatric assessments, despite initially agreeing to undertake such an assessment on the basis that the mother was to do likewise, the father refused to confirm his attendance for the assessment, leading ultimately to both parties’ appointments being cancelled. The father thereby failed to take the step required for reintroduction of time with X. In the witness box, the father admitted that he had known that what would satisfy the mother was a psychiatric assessment, but sought (unsuccessfully) to justify his refusal to undertake one by asserting that the appointment had been “conditional on them providing some evidence to support their claims that I had suddenly developed a mental illness.” Plainly, the psychiatric assessment was itself the means by which the parties would come to know whether the father suffered mental illness. The father’s refusal to undertake the assessment had the entirely foreseeable consequence that his time with X did not resume. As submitted by counsel for the ICL, the father was presented with a clear pathway forward, and his response was not to take it but to stonewall and to take the self-focused approach of refusing to comply with the mother’s reasonable and protective request because he was not getting his own way.
The father did not initiate court proceedings following the cessation of time between himself and X in September 2022. It was the mother who commenced the present proceedings some six months later in March 2023 (a move the father criticised as unnecessary), and even then, the father filed no responding material seeking any time with X until 29 August 2023, being almost six months after the commencement of the proceedings and almost a year after the cessation of time. Although the father did propose mediation,[61] which was an appropriate suggestion, allowing a period of nearly a year to elapse without any time and without taking proactive steps to seek orders from the Court is indicative of a lack of motivation on the father’s behalf with respect to pursuing re-establishment of his relationship with X.
The mother alleged that the father evaded service after she commenced the proceedings. The father asserted that he had not received the mother’s initiating documents, which had been left at his residence, because he had been away working. The father’s explanation, even if true, does not explain his failure to take any action for such a significant period of time. The documents were emailed to the father as early as 21 March 2023. The father’s failure to engage with their contents for a further five months thereafter, particularly in circumstances where he was legally represented from May 2023, suggests that he prioritised making matters costly and difficult for the mother over pursuing the resumption of his relationship with X.
After orders were made on 10 October 2023 providing for supervised time between X and the father, the father did not attend an intake session until 21 December of that year, with the consequence that the commencement of time was delayed until 9 March 2024, period of five months after the order was made (bringing the total period of time since X had seen the father to approximately 18 months). The father attributed this delay to his financial circumstances but adduced no evidence to demonstrate that he had been unable to afford to undertake supervised time in accordance with the orders. In the witness box, he was willing to accept only that his actions had contributed to the delay in him seeing X “in a very small part.”
At the father’s request, the scheduled visits were changed from fortnightly to monthly, with the consequence that time that had been scheduled for X’s third birthday did not take place.
The father also failed to abide by the terms of clear orders regulating the engagement of an expert to prepare a family report, instead attempting to insist on the appointment of an expert of his choice to whom the mother did not agree, leading to a delay of over a year between the making of the order and the preparation of such a report, despite the fact that preparation of the report was the only option reasonably open to the father for putting before the court evidence relevant to the question of whether his time should progress beyond professional supervision and despite the father being represented by a number of lawyers over the relevant period.
On 14 March 2025, the father unilaterally cancelled the arrangement for supervised visits at the CCS, falsely advising the CCS that an alternative arrangement had been agreed.[62] This led to visits which had been scheduled in March and April 2025 being missed. He then wrote to the mother’s solicitor requesting an alternative proposal as supervised visits were “not available” and falsely represented to the mother’s lawyers and to the ICL that the mother’s brother-in-law had expressed a willingness to supervised time between himself and X.[63]
In the witness box, the father implausibly asserted that he had “thought there was an agreement” for the mother’s brother-in-law to act as a supervisor and that he had been “led to believe” that the mother was willing to allow another person to supervise. He adduced no evidence of any communication that could conceivably have led him to hold such a belief. He then criticised the mother for not having asked her brother-in-law to supervise and asserted that “numerous people” were available to supervise, despite the fact that the orders then in place provided for professional supervision. The father implausibly denied that these actions had been an attempt to pressure the mother into agreeing to time taking place outside the CCS. I accept the submission of counsel for the ICL that this conduct was controlling and represented an attempt by the father to force the mother to accept the outcome he wanted. The father’s conduct on this occasion resulted in X not spending any time with the father between 15 February and 28 June 2025.
The mother’s evidence was also that notwithstanding the existence of orders providing for biweekly video calls between X and the father, the father frequently failed to engage in the scheduled calls and ceased undertaking such communication from May 2024, leading ultimately to the discharge of the relevant order in December 2024. The mother reported to Ms N that this had been hard for X, as he had waited for the calls and asked “where’s Dad?” when the father had not participated, becoming upset on occasion.
The father attributed some of his lack of timely engagement and his conduct relating to the identity of the family report writer to financial difficulties. He also emphasised evidence of commitment on his behalf, such as the cost and difficulty associated with his trips to Adelaide to undertake supervised time and his expressed willingness to move to Adelaide to foster his relationship with X. I accept that aspects of the proceedings and the orders have involved expense for the father, particularly the family report, the CCS sessions and the travel to Adelaide for the purpose of spending time.
As will be discussed later in these reasons, however, the father’s level of compliance with his obligations as to financial disclosure was very poor, which limits the extent to which findings can be made about his ability to meet the expenses associated with pursuing and spending time with X. The CCS reports indicate that he travelled to Country R in 2025 which suggests the possibility that he may have some disposable income, but there is limited evidence of the reality of his financial position. In any event, a number of aspects of his lack of engagement cannot be explained by financial constraints.
The father was at pains to emphasise during his closing submissions that he genuinely wants a relationship with X and genuinely believes that it is in X’s best interests to have his father in his life. He submitted that his actions in putting himself through the stress and difficulty of the court proceedings rather than “walking away” demonstrated his commitment to X. The father’s expressions of genuine feelings with respect to his relationship with X are consistent with the reports of him having presented as tearful at times, including when observed by Ms N and at the CCS.
I accept that the father genuinely desires a relationship with X, for X’s benefit and his own. I do not accept the submission made on behalf of the mother to the effect that the father is driven solely by a focus on punishing the mother rather than a focus on X. The fact remains, however, that the father’s commitment to pursuing the relationship with X has been inconsistent. Furthermore, the evidence as a whole supports a finding that the father has been driven, not solely by his desire for a relationship with X, but also by other motivations, such as ensuring that X sees things from his perspective, particularly in relation to what the father considers to have been the mother’s role in the circumstances in which he finds himself, and ensuring that the mother is punished for what he perceives to be her wrongdoings.

Risk of denigration and inappropriate communications from father

Ms N reported that the father presented as unable or unwilling to sufficiently contain his perceptions when in the presence of X, such that he repeatedly made comments which were considered to be inappropriate and not child-focused. A number of examples of such conduct have already been discussed.
Ms N considered it likely that if orders were made for unsupervised time as proposed by the father, X would likely be placed in situations where he was consistently and repeatedly exposed to the father’s perceptions of what transpired in the past and his derogatory narrative about the mother, without a protective adult to intervene as needed. I agree with this assessment. The evidence already discussed demonstrates that the father has persistently demonstrated an inability to prioritise X’s interests over his own negative feelings towards the mother by resisting the urge to criticise or denigrate her in X’s presence despite being aware that doing so is harmful to X. Concerningly, the father has been unable to resist engaging in such behaviour even when he has known that his interactions with X were being observed and reported upon.

The benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with the child's parents, and other people who are significant to the child, where it is safe to do so

A significant aspect of the father’s case was his position that X would benefit from having a relationship with him, and that he did not want X to grow up without a father.
There are some aspects of the evidence which support the conclusion that X would benefit from an ongoing relationship with the father. For example, at a number of the supervised visits, when asked by the CCS worker whether he was ready to spend time with the father, X smiled and said yes. He was also observed to smile and laugh when he saw the father. On one occasion, he entered the playroom, smiling, walked straight to the father and hugged him. On another occasion, he described himself as “really happy” because “I get to play with Daddy.”
X was observed to hug the father on a number of occasions throughout the supervised visits, sometimes but not always upon request from the father. A number of the observations include descriptions of play where X laughed and enjoyed himself and he and the father enjoyed warm interactions. These were described by Ms N as “moments of connection.” At the conclusion of one of the visits, X expressed that he wanted to continue to play with the father.
X was also observed by Ms N to appear excited to see his father and to smile at the prospect of having a play with him, though the father informed Ms N that X had been a lot happier than he usually was and that he was usually more withdrawn and reserved. The father was observed by Ms N to interact in a positive and affectionate manner with X. He was also observed to engage him in conversation, albeit in a manner which limited X’s opportunities to provide him with information.
However, Ms N also observed that X interacted with the father in a manner which suggested that their pattern of interaction may involve X responding in a manner which he had learned and understood was expected by the father. She expressed concern that such a pattern could result in X learning to suppress his own needs in relationships in order to please the other person, possibly leading to mental health problems such as anxiety and depression, and that it could involve poor role modelling, leading X to engage in behaviours such as lashing out or behaving coercively in his own relationships.
Ms N also expressed concerns for X’s relationship with his mother and for his potential experience of stress and confusion, if he were exposed to denigration of her by the father, and the risk that he may adopt poor and antisocial behaviours himself if exposed to dysregulated behaviour by the father towards the mother. The emotional risks to X as already discussed, particularly (but not solely) the risks arising from exposure to his father’s negative view of his mother, are significant.
The father put to Ms N in cross-examination that he, as a fit and energetic person, was better placed than was the mother to meet the needs of an energetic child such as X, particularly when the mother had a newborn. The father could not possibly know how the mother balances the demands of parenting her two children, and newborn status is, in any event, fleeting. However, I accept that different parents have different strengths and offer different benefits to children, and ordinary human experience suggests that it is likely that there are aspects of the father’s interactions with X which could provide him with benefits which are different from and which complement the benefits he derives from his mother’s parenting.
I am satisfied that there are benefits that could be gained by X from the opportunity to have an ongoing relationship with his father. I am not satisfied, however, that these outweigh the significant safety concerns already considered.

Anything else that is relevant to the particular circumstances of the child

Father’s attitude towards mother

The father persistently demonstrated an extremely negative attitude towards the mother and a limited understanding of the importance of her role as X’s primary carer. Some examples of this have already been discussed. Others included the following:
(a) When completing a checklist for Ms N, when asked for X’s longest and best phrases or sentences, the father wrote “mummy makes me cry.” I note in this regard that the father has not spent unsupervised time with X since September 2022, when X was less than 18 months old. None of the reports of their supervised time together record X having said those or similar words to or in the presence of the father.

(b) In response to a question about his own vulnerabilities as a parent, rather than engaging in self-reflection as he had been invited to do, the father told Ms N that he did not want X to grow up “with a silver spoon in his mouth” like the mother, before describing himself favourably as not too harsh and “firm but fair.”

(c) The father told Ms N that the mother was “very clever in manipulating and playing the victim.” He expressed similar sentiments to Dr Begg, to whom he complained of “the lengths to which she has gone to make their post-separation relationship difficult.”

(d) Rather than demonstrating any genuine understanding of the reasons the mother had come to hold concerns about X’s safety and taking steps to reassure her that X was safe in his care, the father persistently dismissed her perspective, asserting that she was suffering “cognitive bias,” was “wilfully ignorant” and that she could not “be reasoned with.”

This attitude presents risks for X, not only of ongoing denigration, but of exposure to conflict and role modelling of poor behaviour, if there were to be time and communication between X and the father.

Poor coparenting relationship

It is apparent from the evidence of both parties that they do not have a functioning coparenting relationship and do not communicate effectively with one another. The father acknowledged in his trial affidavit that communication between the parties is non-existent.
The mother alleged that during the first year following the parties’ separation, during which she was facilitating time between the father and X, the father refused to communicate effectively with her and consistently obstructed her efforts to coparent with him. For example, she alleged that he repeatedly failed to confirm arrangements despite her needing to book flights to City K to facilitate time and unilaterally changed handover times, making the arrangements unnecessarily difficult for her.
The parties’ inability to cooperate extended to the point that they were unable even to agree on the terms of a joint letter of instruction to the single expert psychiatrist and required that the court to settle the letter for them, and were unable to agree on the identity of a family report writer despite the existence of a clear order regulating that process.
The parties’ poor coparenting relationship presents a risk that they would be unable to navigate issues that arose between them in future with respect to parenting arrangements and other issues relating to X, which, particularly combined with the father’s limited ability to regulate his behaviour, poses a risk to X in terms of ongoing exposure to harmful conflict, denigration and poor conduct.

X’s sense of self and identity

Ms N gave evidence that if X did not have his father in his life, this could lead to identify formation issues. She further noted that in the absence of a connection to the father and a sense of who his father is, X might form an idealised perspective of him. The father put to Ms N, and she accepted, that growing up without a father could be problematic for a child, including presenting a risk of the development of mental health problems.
However, Ms N was clear in her opinion that these disadvantages were outweighed by the risks associated with the other matters discussed throughout these reasons, and gave evidence that the protective factors associated with the father were outweighed by the risk factors.
Ms N also opined, and I accept, that the mother had the capacity and child-focus to support X through any such difficulties he may experience in future.

Time and unacceptable risk

It was submitted on behalf of the mother that the father’s concerning conduct, in combination with his lack of insight into its impact on X, constituted an unacceptable risk to X. The ICL, likewise, took the position that time and communication with the father posed an unacceptable risk of harm to X. This risk was said to take the form of risks to X’s emotional safety and the risk of his exposure to the family violence directed by the father to the mother, particularly at handovers.
Although the Court will generally attempt to preserve a relationship between a parent and a child where protective measures can be put in place to manage any risks, there is no presumption that time between a parent and a child is in a child’s best interests.[64] Time may be refused where it would be destructive for the child concerned.[65]
It is not a question of contact for contact’s sake, and if there is a situation where contact with a parent is on balance likely to cause more harm to the child than good, then little purpose is served by the Court making orders for such contact.[66] In McCall & Clark,[67] the Full Court recognised that there will be some cases where there will be no positive benefit to be derived by a child by a court attempting to craft orders to foster a relationship with one parent if this would not be in the child’s best interests.
In each case, the Court must make an independent assessment of what the welfare of the child requires, and contact will only be ordered where there is a prospect of development of a relationship which will be beneficial to the child. It is necessary for the Court to give consideration to the positive as well as negative aspects of the relevant relationship, and balance the risks associated with facilitating the relationship against any benefits that may be derived from doing so.[68] Severance of a relationship between a parent and a child must be proportionate to the risk.[69]
As Tree J held in Theophane & Hunt (Final Parenting Orders):[70]
Plainly it is a serious matter to order that a child neither spend time with nor communicate with a parent. Such orders properly ought be restricted to cases where that outcome is plainly mandated in the best interests of the child, and no other regime of orders is appropriate or workable. Such orders are commonly employed where the Court is satisfied that a parent poses an unacceptable risk of harm to a child, which cannot be ameliorated by supervision of time and communication between that parent and the child. ...
In particular, it is well established that a court will not make an order for a child to live with, or spend time with, a parent if it would expose the child to an unacceptable risk.[71] Although the test initially related to allegations of risk of harm from sexual abuse, it has subsequently been extended to cases involving allegations of children being at risk of physical or emotional harm for other reasons.[72] In Vasser & Taylor-Black,[73] the Full Court referred to the decision of the High Court of Australia (“the High Court”) in M & M,[74] in which the test was laid out, as “having become the ‘touchstone’ setting out the relevant principles to be applied in cases of asserted unacceptable risk of any kind.” In addition to assessing past events, the Court must separately assess future risk. This is a predictive or prospective exercise.[75]
In N & S & The Separate Representative,[76] Fogarty J said:
Thus, the essential importance of the unacceptable risk question as I see it is in its direction to judges to give real and substantial consideration to the facts of the case, and to decide whether or not, and why or why not, those facts could be said to raise an unacceptable risk of harm to the child. Thus, the value of the expression is not in a magical provision of an appropriate standard, but in its direction to Judges to consider deeply where the facts of a particular case fall, and explain adequately their findings in this regard.
The assessment of whether there is an unacceptable risk requires determination on the evidence whether there is a risk, the magnitude of that risk and the potential consequences of the harm that might befall the child or children concerned.[77] This involves giving consideration to the extent to which any risk found to exist may be ameliorated by protective measures such as supervision.[78] If an unacceptable risk is found, the Court must take steps which are proportionate to the degree of the risk.[79]
In Pascoe & O’Keefe and Ors[80] the Full Court emphasised the significance of family violence to the assessment of risk:
... In cross-examination, the single expert discussed the deleterious psychological consequences for children of their exposure to family violence, which evidence was hardly a revelation. This Court has long-recognised its destructive effect on the immediate victim and those who are exposed to the violence including, most relevantly in this case, the child.

(citations omitted)

Ms N expressed concern that if unsupervised time were to be ordered as proposed by the father, X may learn to inhibit expressing his own needs while acting in ways which meet his father's needs or expectations, X's sense of security in his relationship with his mother may be undermined, and more broadly X may be exposed to a greater sense of the world being unsafe than is appropriate or manageable for a child of his young age, in turn adversely impacting upon his developmental trajectory.
This, together with the concerns already discussed, led Ms N to urge a cautious approach to time spending between X and the father.
I accept the submission made on behalf of the ICL that Ms N’s evidence, together with the father’s, demonstrated a lack of insight on his part into the harmful nature of his conduct and its impact on X, which suggests that there is no reasonable prospect of a reduction in the risk posed to X if time or communication were to be ordered.
I have carefully turned my mind to the existence of the possible risks of harm and also to the magnitude of the possible harm. In light of the findings I have made, I am satisfied that there is an unacceptable risk that X will suffer emotional harm and that his experience of safe and consistent parenting will be undermined if orders are made which provide for him to spend time with the father.

Amelioration of risk

I have considered whether the risk to X might be ameliorated by protective measures. It was the position of the mother and the ICL that the risk posed to X by the father was so great that no conditions or injunctions could ameliorate it.
One potential means of ameliorating the risk would be ongoing supervision. Ms N recommended that time between them remain professionally supervised at a CCS on the basis that intervention would occur to protect X should the father’s behaviour not be considered child-focused. Whilst it remains a matter of discretion, however, many authorities have expressed caution in relation to the desirability of orders providing for supervision for indefinite periods.[81] Furthermore, it is an agreed fact that the CCS staff have indicated that they are not able to offer any further supervised visits to this family, and there is no evidence before the court of any other available and suitable supervisor.
Furthermore, I accept the submission made on behalf of the mother that the evidence of the father’s inability to regulate his behaviour in the face of frustration, which has already manifested in inappropriate behaviour towards professional supervisors, suggests that he may be unable to tolerate the constraints associated with long-term supervision, which would put X at further risk.
In any event, Ms N recommended that if the court accepted the mother’s account of the history of her experiences (and those of X) with the father’s behaviour, or that the father was unable or unwilling to act in ways which were child-focused and placed X’s needs at the centre of his decision-making, both of which are findings which have been made, no time spending with the father may be in X’s best interests.
I note that the father’s inability to control his behaviour, and his lack of insight into its harmful consequences, has meant that even in the context of reportable professional supervision, he has proven himself unable to regulate his behaviour, or to prioritise X’s needs over his own feelings, particularly his negative views of the mother, to the extent necessary to protect X from the risk of emotional harm. These same factors militate against any reasonable expectation of it being safe to progress to unsupervised time in the foreseeable future, which undermines any likely utility in maintaining an ongoing arrangement of supervised time.
Ms N further opined that an order for therapeutic measures as a precondition to the reintroduction of time would be unlikely to be effective, because successful therapeutic intervention would require not only internal motivation for change, but the father taking responsibility for past wrongdoings and an understanding of what was being worked towards. Whilst the father’s participation in Circle of Security and engagement of a life coach demonstrate willingness to engage, the tenor of the vast majority of his evidence and his ongoing conduct suggest that he does not have a meaningful understanding of what it is that would need to be addressed in order to ensure X’s emotional safety.
The evidence also supports a finding that the imposition of injunctions will be insufficient to protect X and the mother from the father’s harmful behaviour if there is to be time and communication between X and the father in future. In particular, as submitted on behalf of the mother, the father’s continued breaches of the family violence order demonstrate clearly that the father cannot control himself sufficiently to comply with orders, with the consequence that the mother and X cannot be protected by them.
The evidence does not support a finding that the identified risks to X can sufficiently be ameliorated by any protective measures.

Communication with father

Ms N recommended videocalls “in between face-to-face time spending” should X wish to do so and should this be able to occur in an emotionally safe and child-focused manner. In the witness box, she emphasised the conditional nature of this recommendation.
In light of the substantial evidence of the father’s harmful communications to X and in his presence, including but not limited to harmful communications during video calls, I am not satisfied that such communication could occur in a safe and child-focused manner.
The ICL submitted, and I accept, that other means of communication, such as letters, gifts and cards, are also unlikely to promote X’s best interests or promote his emotional safety in circumstances in which the father has demonstrated that he is willing use such communications as a means of engaging in abusive and inappropriate communications directed towards the mother and denigration of the mother to X and has not even been deterred from doing so by the existence of a family violence order.
There will be no order for communication between X and the father.

Injunctions

Each of the parties sought injunctions as part of their applications with respect to X. The power to grant injunctions is found in subsection 68B(1) of the Act, which provides, in summary, that the Court may the court may make such order or grant such injunction as it considers appropriate for the welfare of the child. Although the paramountcy principle in section 60CA does not apply to the granting of an injunction, X’s best interests are a relevant consideration.[82]
The mother sought injunctions restraining the father from attending within 100 metres of her residence or workplace or identified residences of maternal family members, attending within 100 metres of her or X, attending within 100 metres of X’s childcare, kindergarten or school, or communicating with her or X.
The mother deposed to the father having attended at her parents’ home and that of her sister in the past without notice or consent. The evidence already considered includes (inter alia) evidence of the father attempting to pressure X’s childcare centre into consenting to him collecting X despite not being permitted to do so; sending inappropriate and harmful messages to X and/or the mother in the guise of gifts and cards; sending the mother a photograph of herself and X taken by a person who was a stranger to her with, on the interpretation most generous to the father, no regard for the likely impact on the mother of so doing; being present outside a residence where she was staying in breach of a family violence order; and various other repeated breaches of the family violence order. When this conduct is considered together with my finding that it is not in X’s best interests to have time or communication with the father as a result of an unacceptable risk to his safety, I am satisfied that the injunctions sought by the mother are appropriate for his welfare.
I have turned my mind to whether, to the extent that they relate to the same matters to which the family violence order relates, subsection 114AB(2) provides a barrier to the making of the injunctions sought by the mother. However, in light of the fact that the family violence order was brought on the application of the police, and not the mother herself, I am satisfied that this court retains jurisdiction to make the injunctions.[83] In any event, the operation of subsection 114AB would not operate to preclude the application made by the ICL in the same terms.
The father sought injunctions restraining each of the parties from (in summary) denigrating the other or discussing these proceedings with X or in his presence. The mother gave evidence that she agreed to these injunctions irrespective of whether the orders were to provide for the father to spend time or communicate with X. I am satisfied that such injunctions are appropriate for X’s welfare.

Passport and travel

The mother sought an order enabling her to obtain a passport for X without the need for the father’s involvement. This was submitted to be a practical measure designed to enable operation of the order for sole decision-making, which would avoid the need for her to make a further court application in future. In light of the father’s inappropriate conduct with respect to his communications both with the mother and with her solicitors and the parties’ inability to coparent, as outlined throughout these reasons, I accept that it is likely that court further proceedings would otherwise be required. Avoiding this is consistent both with the overarching purpose set out in section 95 of the Act and with X’s best interests.
Furthermore, in light of the findings made above concerning the father’s conduct and the effect of the other orders that are to be made, X’s best interests would be promoted by avoiding the need for the mother and X to be beholden to the father with respect to issues relating to a passport and travel. X’s best interests will be promoted by measures enabling him to have the opportunity to travel overseas. The orders with respect to overseas travel and obtaining a passport will be made as sought by the mother.

Orders to be made

For all of the reasons outlined above, orders will be made in the terms as set out at the commencement of these reasons, which orders I am satisfied are in X’s best interests.

PROPERTY

The parties’ applications

The wife sought, in summary, that the net non-superannuation assets of the parties be divided in proportions of 60 per cent to her and 40 per cent to the husband. She sought that this be effected by her retention of the entirety of the sale proceeds of the former matrimonial home, being the property at S Street, City K, South Australia (which are currently being held on behalf of the parties in a conveyancer’s trust account), the husband making a cash payment to her, the husband retaining the property at G Street, Town H (“the [Town H] property”), a boat and trailer owned by the parties which is currently in the possession of the husband being sold (this aspect of her application having been orally amended at trial), and the parties each otherwise keeping all assets in their respective possession.
The husband sought, in summary, that the net assets of the parties (including superannuation) be divided in proportions of 60 per cent to him and 40 per cent to the wife. He proposed that this be effected by the sale proceeds of the former matrimonial home being divided equally between the parties, him retaining the Town H property, the wife taking possession of and retaining the boat and trailer, and the parties each otherwise retaining the various assets in their respective possession.
Neither party proposed that there be a superannuation split.

The legal framework

Orders under section 79 of the Act altering the property interests of the parties to a marriage may only be made if the court is satisfied, pursuant to subsection 79(2), that it is just and equitable to make such orders.[84] The court must not start with an assumption that one party or the other has the right to have their property divided between them or that the parties’ rights to or interests in property should be different from those that presently exist.[85] Although the court must not conflate its determination pursuant to subsection 79(2) of the Act with its determination pursuant to subsections 79(4) and (5), it is permissible to have regard to the factors contained within those subsections when considering the justice and equity of making property settlement orders.[86]
The court must identify the existing legal and equitable rights and interests in any property of the parties to the marriage or either of them and their existing liabilities.[87] If the court determines that it is just and equitable for the property interests of the parties to be altered, subsections 79(4) and (5) set out the matters the court must take into account in considering what order, if any, should be made.[88] The court must identify and assess the parties' contributions within the meaning of subsections 79(4)(a)-(c) and then take account of the relevant matters referred to in subsections 79(4)(ca)-(g) and 79(5). The court must be satisfied that both the percentage-based division arrived at and the orders themselves are just and equitable.[89]

Husband’s non-disclosure

There were significant difficulties with the husband’s level of compliance with his disclosure obligations throughout the proceedings. The obligations of parties to financial proceedings are set out in section 71B of the Act, which (relevantly) provides as follows:
71B Duty of disclosure
Duty of disclosure in proceedings
(1) Each party to a proceeding relating to financial or property matters of a marriage (other than proceedings on appeal) has a duty to the court and to each other party to give full and frank disclosure, in a timely manner, of all information and documents relevant to:
(a) for a party to the marriage—the issues in the proceeding that relate to financial or property matters of the marriage; or
...
(2) The duty under subsection (1) applies from the start of the proceeding and continues until the proceeding is finalised.
Note: Courts have a range of powers that may be exercised to impose consequences when a person fails to comply with their duty of disclosure. For example, a court might do any of the following:
(a) take the failure into account when making an order under section 79 (alteration of property interests);

(b) make any orders with respect to costs or security for costs against the person that the court considers just, having regard to the failure;

(c) make any orders with respect to disclosure that the court considers appropriate;

(d) if an order made by the court is contravened—impose sanctions under section 112AD;

(e) punish the person under section 112AP for contempt;

(f) stay or dismiss all or part of the proceedings.

...

Financial or property matters of the marriage
(7) Any of the following matters, so far as they relate to a marriage, are financial or property matters of the marriage:
(a) financial matters;
(b) matters that are or might become the subject of proceedings under any of the following provisions of this Act:
(i) this Part (orders with respect to spousal maintenance or the property of the parties to the marriage);

...

Relevant information and documents
(8) A party’s duty to disclose information and documents is a duty to disclose information known to the party and documents that are or have been in the possession or under the control of the party.

(9) A party’s duty to disclose information and documents includes any information or documents prescribed by the applicable Rules of Court for the purposes of the duty.

Note: The duty to disclose is not limited to prescribed information and documents. The applicable Rules of Court may also prescribe other matters in relation to the duty of disclosure.
Further details of the disclosure obligations imposed on parties to proceedings under the Act are set out in chapter 6 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”).
The wife deposed to substantial failures on the part of the husband to comply with his obligations with respect to disclosure (despite numerous requests and court orders) and to obstructive and other difficult behaviour on his part with respect to the preparation of the financial aspects of the proceedings. As at the date of her trial affidavit, the wife deposed that she had received no financial disclosure from the husband since December 2023.
Despite having filed an undertaking as to disclosure, the husband adduced no evidence to contradict the wife’s allegations as to his recalcitrance with respect to disclosure and did not respond to the relevant passages of her trial affidavit. In the witness box, he refuted an allegation that he had been evasive with respect to disclosure and said that he had been “useless,” which does not constitute an excuse. I accept the wife’s evidence in this regard.
It is worth repeating that the husband was represented by no less than nine separate firms of lawyers at various times during the course of the proceedings prior to the commencement of the trial. In such circumstances, it is inconceivable that he could have been unaware of his obligations.
The husband was permitted to tender some financial documents which were produced by him during the trial, notwithstanding the operation of rule 6.17(a) of the Rules. Notwithstanding this, his compliance with his disclosure obligations remained lacking.
The husband’s obstructive conduct with respect to the financial aspects of the proceedings extended beyond simply failing to provide adequate disclosure. It included refusal to cooperate with obtaining a valuation of the parties’ boat, including by claiming that he was unable to make it available for inspection (ultimately leading to a “desktop” valuation being obtained),[90] and refusing to allow the appointed real property valuer to access the Town H property, requiring the valuer to walk around the perimeter and rely on photographs supplied by the wife.[91]
Despite the claim that he was unable to make the boat available, the husband subsequently purported to invoice the wife for “boat storage” at the property at which he resides before deposing in his trial affidavit that it had been “in long term storage at a mutual friend[’]s house for the majority of the time.”
The husband asserted by way of a Financial Statement filed by him on 29 August 2023 that he had sold Motor Vehicle 3 in June 2022 for $3,000 and a camper trailer in 2022 for $6,000. He subsequently asserted in correspondence sent to the wife’s solicitors on 16 May 2024 that he intended to sell the camper trailer, clearly implying that he still had it. This was confirmed when the trailer was made available by the husband for a valuation in September 2024.
In the witness box, the husband insisted that he had sold the items when he had originally said he had, saying that he had sold them for cash and “nobody gets a receipt.” He said that by the time of the correspondence in May 2024, he had “regained possession” of the camper trailer because the person to whom he had sold it had found that it had not suited his needs, and that this had occurred a few months after it had been sold. The husband provided no coherent explanation for why, in light of his evidence that he had “regained” the camper trailer shortly after selling it in 2022, he had sworn a Financial Statement in August 2023 in which he asserted that he had sold it in 2022 but had not disclosed that it had been “regained.” This evidence was wholly unsatisfactory and cannot be accepted.
The Financial Statement relied on by the husband at trial (which had been prepared by his former solicitor and signed by him at a time when he was legally represented) was deficient in many respects. By way of one example only, it failed to disclose any income or expenses whatsoever at Parts D and G respectively (although Part N had been completed). It also failed to disclose assets the existence of which is not disputed, such as the husband’s interests in the proceeds of sale of the former matrimonial home and the boat. As a consequence, this document was unreliable and of no real assistance to the court.
As a consequence of the husband’s unsatisfactory level of compliance with his disclosure obligations, his obstructive conduct with respect to the financial aspects of the proceedings more broadly, and his unsatisfactory evidence, the evidence available to assist the Court with determining the financial aspects of the proceedings has been limited, and I have been required to do the best I can with unsatisfactory and incomplete evidence. The width of the range of consequences available to the Court in such circumstances is outlined in rule 6.17 and in a number of well-known authorities.[92] The lack of satisfactory evidence to assist with determining issues in dispute arising from the husband’s recalcitrance should not be interpreted in his favour. Any such uncertainties will therefore be resolved in favour of the wife.

Assets and liabilities

At the commencement of the trial, the parties jointly tendered a joint balance sheet,[93] which set out the items (and their values) contended for by each party and the areas of agreement. Further amendments were made to each of the parties’ positions orally at the hearing.

Issues to be determined

Boat and trailer

The parties own a boat, which is registered in joint names. It has an associated trailer which is registered in the sole name of the wife. Although the joint balance sheet initially indicated that there was an agreed value for the boat, the Court was informed that that value was no longer agreed by the wife. Although the reason for the late change to the wife’s position was not clear, the basis for her unwillingness to accept the valuation was asserted to be the lack of reliability of the valuation arising from the husband’s obstructive behaviour surrounding its preparation, as already discussed.
The wife sought the sale of the boat and trailer, though in her closing submissions, her senior counsel also indicated that she would be content with the husband retaining them. The husband indicated that he did not wish to retain the boat, but he opposed the sale and sought that the wife retain the boat and trailer. His expressed reasons for this were that the boat is located in Town J (which is where he lives) and that he lacks the means to tow it, so it would be difficult to transport it.
Generally speaking, where neither party wishes to retain an asset, an order for its sale will be the appropriate order.[94] Although this is a matter for the court’s discretion, I do not consider there to be any basis in the current matter for requiring either party to retain an asset they do not wish to retain. This is particularly so in the context of the issues with the valuation as identified by the wife.
The husband was plainly able to make arrangements for the boat to be taken to its present location (and on his evidence, has found a way to move it on at least two occasions). I do not accept that he is unable to do so again. In light of the history of the relationship between the parties, and particularly the husband’s conduct towards the wife, it is not appropriate to require her to attend at the husband’s residence to collect the boat. The orders will provide for the boat to be taken by the husband to F Company to facilitate its sale, as sought by the wife.

Camper trailer

The husband asserted that the camper trailer owned by him had been sold (again). The wife alleged that the husband still had the camper trailer and sought its inclusion at the value of $10,000. As I have already mentioned, the husband has given contradictory and unsatisfactory explanations in relation to this and has not provided documentary evidence to support his assertions. I do not accept his evidence of having disposed of the camper trailer. It will be included as sought by the wife.

Motor Vehicle 2

The husband opposed the inclusion of his Motor Vehicle 2. He asserted in his closing submissions that this car had been “written off” and was “not in existence.” He adduced no evidence of this, other than evidence given under cross-examination that he does not currently own a car. It was not included in his Financial Statement, though as I have already mentioned, that document was incomplete and unreliable. I do not accept the husband’s evidence with respect to this vehicle. The wife sought to include this vehicle at the value of $17,890. The vehicle will be included as sought by the wife.

Husband’s Motor Vehicle 3

As already discussed, the husband gave inconsistent and contradictory stories with respect to his Motor Vehicle 3 and the question of whether it had been sold. He failed to adduce supporting documentary evidence. I do not accept his evidence with respect to this asset. The wife sought to include this asset at the value of $3,000. It will be included.

Wife’s motor vehicles

The parties agreed that the wife had Motor Vehicle 1 which should be included at a value of $59,980.
The husband also sought to include Motor Vehicle 4 and Motor Vehicle 5, both of which he asserted were owned by the wife. He variously asserted that the combined value of those vehicles was $15,000 and $52,000. The husband asserted that these vehicles had existed at the time of separation and he did not know what had happened to them.
The wife’s evidence in this regard was that in March 2025, her Motor Vehicle 4 had suffered engine failure and required a new engine, the cost of which would have exceeded the value of the vehicle. As a consequence, she sent the car to the wreckers’ and purchased Motor Vehicle 1. That evidence is plausible and I accept it.
Motor Vehicle 5 was a vehicle which the husband accepted the wife had purchased for her use in City K when she frequently travelled there to facilitate X’s time with him between the parties’ separation and September 2022. He accepted that the wife had purchased the vehicle for the modest sum of $5,000 and was unable to dispute that she had sold it for a similarly modest sum once she no longer needed it as she was no longer travelling to City K. The evidence does not establish that there remains a Motor Vehicle 5 in the wife’s possession.

Wine collection

The husband sought the inclusion of a wine collection in the wife’s possession with an asserted value of $15,000. He adduced no evidence of the existence of such a wine collection, much less its value. The wine collection will not be included.

Musical Instrument

At the commencement of the trial, the husband asserted that the wife had a musical instrument worth $9,000, which he sought to include. There was no mention in his trial affidavit of the existence of a musical instrument, and there is no valuation evidence before the court with respect to a musical instrument.
The wife gave oral evidence that she had not played the musical instrument since she was a young teenager, that she had previously played one owned by her grandmother who had since passed away, and that she had not inherited the musical instrument.
The husband has not established that the wife owns a musical instrument.

Ring

As with the musical instrument, the husband raised the existence of a ring at the commencement of the trial without having adduced any evidence as to its existence or value. He subsequently tendered a valuation certificate dated 12 February 2018[95] and sought that the ring be included at the value set out in that certificate, being $8,000. The wife gave credible evidence, which I accept, that she had been given a ring by the husband when he proposed to her, but it had been lost at around the time of their separation. I am not satisfied that either of the parties is currently in possession of the ring and will not include it.

Wife’s bank account

A further amended version of the balance sheet was tendered by the wife at the conclusion of the trial.[96] The only significant departure from the joint balance sheet was a change in the wife’s bank account balance, which had reduced from $98,067 to $1,438.28. This was asserted to be explained by expenditure on legal fees. This was broadly consistent with the figures provided in costs notices filed on behalf of the wife ahead of the commencement of the trial and its final day. This was also consistent with common sense, noting both the costs of litigation and the fact that the wife was on parental leave at the time of commencement of the trial. The balance of the wife’s bank account will be included at $1,438.28.

Funds accessed at separation

Shortly prior to the parties’ separation, the wife withdrew the sum of $65,228 from the parties’ home loan, which she deposed represented excess contributions she had made to that loan during the relationship. She deposed to having done so in order to have funds to use for living expenses while she remained on parental leave. The husband sought that this be treated as an “addback.” Following the Full Court’s decision in Shinohara & Shinohara,[97] the practice of adding property back as notional property is no longer available, and instead funds accessed by a party prior to a final property settlement should be considered pursuant to subsection 79(5) of the Act. This issue is therefore discussed later in these reasons.

Loan owed to husband’s mother

The husband sought to include a loan owed to his mother with an asserted balance of $9,000. He adduced no evidence of the existence of such a loan (other than an unparticularised reference to it in his unreliable Financial Statement) and no evidence as to the purpose of this loan, its terms, or when the funds had been borrowed. He called no evidence from his mother with respect to this loan and adduced no documentary evidence demonstrating his receipt of such funds. I am not satisfied of the existence of the loan. It will not be included.

Legal fees

Both the joint balance sheet and the updated version tendered by the wife included a figure for “legal fees to be paid” as a liability owed by the wife. Inclusion of this item was sensibly not pressed by her at the conclusion of the trial.
