Whether it is just and equitable that orders be made

Having identified the parties’ existing legal and equitable interest in property, I now turn to consider whether it is just and equitable to make orders altering those interests. In this matter, the parties agree that it is just and equitable that orders be made altering their interests in their property. However, that alone is insufficient. The court must itself be satisfied that it is just and equitable that an order be made.
The parties in this matter no longer live together and are no longer in a relationship but their finances remain intertwined, including trust funds which remain held on behalf of both of them pending the resolution of these proceedings. Consistently with section 81 of the Act, each party has a legitimate desire to end their financial relationship. If there were no adjustment to the legal ownership of their property interests, there would be no justice and equity to the parties. This is clearly one of the “vast majority of cases” referred to by the plurality in the High Court in Stanford & Stanford,[98] in which the requirements of subsection 79(2) of the Act are readily satisfied. It is plainly just and equitable to make an order pursuant to section 79 of the Act in these proceedings for a division of property between the parties.

Treatment of superannuation

The wife approached the trial on the basis that the parties’ superannuation interests should be treated as a separate pool from their non-superannuation assets, with the non-superannuation pool to be adjusted in the proportions as sought by her and the superannuation to “lie where it falls.” The husband sought that the parties’ superannuation and non-superannuation interests be treated as a single pool.
The approach taken by the wife, whilst not mandatory, is consistent with authority including, in particular, the Full Court’s decision in Coghlan & Coghlan.[99] I agree that such an approach is just and equitable in the circumstances of this case.
The wife deposed to having made substantial contributions to her superannuation in the post-separation period, after having left the parties’ relationship with a similar superannuation balance to that of the husband. As is discussed in greater detail below, these post-separation contributions made by the wife were not matched by post-separation contributions of other types made by the husband.
I note also that the parties are both relatively young and have many years of earning capacity ahead of them, each has considerable superannuation interests when compared with the overall size of their asset pool, and the disparity between their superannuation interests is not significant.
I am therefore satisfied that the approach urged by the wife will do justice and equity between the parties.

Contributions

Contributions at commencement of relationship

It is well established that if a party brings significant property into a relationship that is considered to be very important consideration.[100] In considering such contributions, it is necessary to trace the use of those assets and consider the foundation that they laid for the subsequent accumulation of wealth by the parties.[101] Contribution of a particular asset may be a significant in the context of the future accumulation of the wealth of the parties.[102] It is necessary to have regard to the context of such contributions, and specifically, to the opportunity that they created and their impact on the subsequent wealth of the parties as at the date of the hearing.[103]
However, a disparity in initial contributions may be diminished by the length of the relationship and the contributions of the parties during the course of the relationship.[104] The weight to be attached to initial contributions must be assessed against the rubric of all financial and non-financial contributions made by the parties throughout the relationship as a whole.[105]
At the commencement of the parties’ relationship, the wife owned Motor Vehicle 1 worth approximately $6,000, which was sold in 2016 for $3,100; and held a Higher Education Contribution Scheme debt of $7,000.
At the commencement of the parties’ relationship, the husband owned:
(a) Vacant land at T Street, Town J (“the [Town J] property”), which had been purchased for approximately $85,000 in January 2012, and which was subject to a mortgage of unknown quantum. There was no retrospective valuation evidence with respect to this property. It was sold and produced net sale proceeds of $46,000 in 2021.

(b) The Town H property, which had been purchased for approximately $45,000 between 2009 and 2012. There was no retrospective valuation evidence with respect to this property. The husband estimated that it was worth $180,000 at the commencement of the relationship, which is also its current value. This appears unlikely, as it would mean that there was a very significant increase in its value in the short time between its purchase and the commencement of the parties’ relationship, and then no increase over the significant period thereafter. This property has remained largely untouched during the relationship.

(c) Motor Vehicle 6, which had been purchased for $10,000 in 2013 (and was sold in 2020 for $5,000).

Although the initial contributions made by the husband cannot be quantified with specificity, it cannot be doubted that they represent a substantial contribution. The Town H property, in particular, represents a significant component of the pool of assets presently in existence. These contributions must be given substantial weight.

Contributions during the relationship

The evidence of both parties reveals that they each made financial and non-financial contributions during the “economic partnership”[106] of their marriage.
Both parties worked in paid employment during their relationship, though the husband spent some brief periods without employment, including a period of approximately four months in 2019 when he was unable to work as a result of injuries sustained in an accident, and the wife took parental leave for X’s birth and was still on that leave when the parties separated. The wife asserted that she had been the primary income earner during the parties’ relationship. This was consistent with the husband’s evidence.
The parties operated a joint bank account, to which they made roughly equal contributions (though the wife deposed to having made greater contributions than the husband at times), and that account was used to pay for shared expenses such as rent, groceries and utility bills.
The husband deposed to his parents having contributed $10,000 to the cost of the parties’ wedding in October 2019.
In early 2020, the parties purchased their former matrimonial home for $550,000. The wife deposed that the purchase was made using the sum of $139,136.39 which had been saved by her during the parties’ relationship, together with the entirety of the stamp duty and other acquisition costs, and the parties jointly obtained mortgage finance to fund the balance of the purchase price.
The husband asserted that he had agreed to the purchase of the wife’s “dream house” on the basis that she pay for it.
The wife deposed to having made greater financial contributions towards joint expenses, including mortgage repayments, than did the husband from March 2020 until the parties’ separation.
The husband contributed labour towards renovations and improvements undertaken at the former matrimonial home, including landscaping, redoing the driveway, electrical works, and the removal and replacement of a retaining wall. The wife deposed to having made the greater financial contribution towards these renovations and improvements. The husband asserted that these improvements had “dramatically increased” the value of the property, but there was no expert evidence before the court to that effect.
It is apparent from the evidence of both parties that the wife undertook the majority of parenting responsibilities following X’s birth, and undertook most homemaking duties, while the husband undertook most of the outdoor tasks such as gardening.
The Town J property, which had been owned by the husband at the commencement of the parties’ relationship, was sold in early 2021, producing net sale proceeds of $46,000. The wife deposed to the husband having paid $11,000 of these proceeds to the mortgage encumbering the former matrimonial home and otherwise having retained the balance of $35,000 for his sole use and benefit. The husband alleged that he had sold the property at a loss “under pressure from” the wife to allow for “some money” to be put towards the former matrimonial home but gave no specific evidence of the quantum of the sale proceeds applied to that purchase.
The husband asserted that he had moved interstate (initially to Darwin and then to Western Australia, prior to the parties’ return to South Australia) in order to support the wife’s career, at the expense of his own. The wife’s evidence was that both parties’ employment circumstances were enhanced by their moves. The evidence before the court when considered as a whole suggests that the husband’s work history has been, and continues to be, varied and unpredictable. The husband’s poor compliance with his disclosure obligations and evasive evidence when cross-examined about the circumstances of his employment precludes a finding of the type sought by him. The evidence does not support a finding of any sacrifice or relinquishment made in this regard by the husband by virtue of the parties’ interstate moves.

Post-separation contributions

Following the parties’ separation, the husband continued to reside in the former matrimonial home. He tried to distance himself from this by saying that he was often away for work and suggesting that it was his “by default.” I do not accept this argument. Firstly, the husband’s evidence as to his work arrangements was evasive and unsatisfactory. Secondly, whether he was there full time or not, he was the party with the sole use of the property as his home. Thirdly, this had come about in circumstances in which the wife had proposed that he vacate the property so that she could reside there, and he had refused.
Notwithstanding the husband’s ongoing occupation of the property, the wife made the full mortgage payments in September and October 2021, and following a fifty per cent reduction in the repayments based on the wife’s parental leave, she made half of the reduced payments from November 2021 until March 2022.
The husband paid approximately $3,006.74 to have the hot water service replaced.[107]
The wife’s Motor Vehicle 1 was acquired by her following separation.
The wife has made very significant post-separation parenting contributions without assistance (financial or otherwise), and indeed in the face of obstruction from, the husband.
The husband claimed to have made “enormous” contributions towards X’s mental and physical wellbeing by funding his own expenses to travel to Adelaide to spend time with him. That submission is unsustainable in light of the findings with respect to the impact of the husband’s behaviour on X. In any event, any financial contribution made by the husband towards X’s wellbeing following separation is significantly overshadowed by the contributions made by the wife in this regard.

Assessment of contributions

In Aleksovski & Aleksovski,[108] Kay J held as follows:
It is ... necessary that trial Judges weigh and assess the contributions of all kinds and from all sources made by each of the parties throughout the period of their cohabitation and then translate such assessment into a percentage of the overall property of the parties or provide for a transfer of property in specie in accordance with that assessment.
His Honour went on to say that “what is important is to somehow give a reasonable value to all of the elements that go to making up the entirety of the marriage relationship.”
More recently, the Full Court in Jabour & Jabour[109] emphasised the importance of giving recognition to the myriad of contributions that each of the parties has made during the course of their relationship. The court is not required to dissect each individual contribution and attach a percentage to it and should not take a mathematical approach to the calculation of contributions.[110] Rather, the court must make a holistic assessment, taking into account the contributions of all types made by the parties over the duration of their relationship.[111]
It was the wife’s position that the parties’ contributions should be assessed as having been equal. The husband asserted by way of his case outline that their contributions should be assessed as having been made 90 per cent by him and 10 per cent by the wife, though this did not align with his overall proposal. In his closing submissions, he asserted that his case was predominantly (though not entirely) based on an assertion that he had made greater contributions than had the wife.

Other subsection 79(4) factors

(ca) the effect of any family violence, to which one party to the marriage has subjected or exposed the other party, on the ability of a party to the marriage to make the kind of contributions referred to in paragraphs (a), (b) and (c)

In relation to this factor, I refer to the findings earlier in these reasons as to the family violence to which the wife was (and has continued to be) subjected by the husband. There was limited direct evidence before the Court as to the effect of that family violence on the wife’s ability to make contributions. Her ability to make financial contributions would appear on the evidence not to have been impacted. However, the impact of family violence on contributions may be inferred,[112] and in the particular circumstances of this case, the evidence supports an inference that the wife’s parenting contributions have been adversely impacted by the husband’s family violence towards her.

(d) the effect of any proposed order upon the earning capacity of either party to the marriage

No likely impact of the proposed orders on either party’s earning capacity has been identified.

(g) any child support under the Child Support (Assessment) Act 1989 that a party to the marriage has provided for a child of the marriage

The husband has paid no child support for X. He deposed that he pays for private health insurance for X, but documents tendered by him suggest that this has not been the case at least since October 2024.[113] He was critical of the wife for having obtained a separate private health insurance policy for X, but it is clear from the husband’s ongoing pattern of obstructive and difficult conduct towards the wife that it is not realistic to expect her to rely on being able to claim against a policy held and controlled by the husband. The policy held by the husband therefore provides no actual benefit to either the wife or X, even if it continues to exist.

Conclusion as to subsection 79(4) factors

In my view, having regard to the considerations outlined above, the various factors outlined in subsection 79(4) should result in an assessment of 60 per cent to the husband and 40 per cent to the wife.

Current and future circumstances

As with the assessment of contributions, the court is not obliged to give separate weighting to, or to analyse, each relevant subsection 79(5) factor with mathematical precision.[114] The court may disregard any of the factors which are irrelevant, and may limit its consideration to those which are most relevant.[115]

Consideration of effect of contributions assessment

It is necessary to consider the effect of the findings as to contribution on the respective positions of the parties before proceeding to determine whether any adjustment is warranted pursuant to subsection 79(5).[116] In the present case, there is nothing to suggest that the Court should not proceed to an assessment under subsection 79(5).

Subsection 79(5) considerations

The factors enumerated in subsection 79(5) which are relevant to the present proceedings are discussed in the following paragraphs.

(a) the effect of any family violence, to which one party to the marriage has subjected or exposed the other party, on the current and future circumstances of the other party, including on any of the matters mentioned elsewhere in this subsection

The evidence does not establish that the family violence to which I have found the wife was subjected by the husband has had or will have ongoing impacts on her current and future circumstances. This is particularly the case in circumstances in which the orders will not provide for ongoing interactions between them.

(b) the age and state of health of each of the parties to the marriage

The husband deposed that he had “never fully recovered” from the accident he suffered in 2019. He adduced no medical evidence to support his assertion in this regard nor any particulars of ongoing difficulties in this regard. The parties are otherwise of similar ages and there is no evidence to support a finding that either of them suffers any ongoing health concerns.

(c) the income, property and financial resources of each of the parties to the marriage and the physical and mental capacity of each of them for appropriate gainful employment

The court is required to have regard to the earning capacity of each of the parties, not simply their current income.[117]
Each of the parties has the capacity for gainful employment. At the time of making her trial affidavit, the wife was on parental leave. She had previously been working four days per week. Her taxable income for the financial year ending 30 June 2025 was $317,041. She deposed to an intention to return to work at approximately two and a half days per week from November 2025. By the time of the conclusion of the trial, she had returned to work on a part-time basis. The husband “anticipated” that the wife would go back to working full time in the future, but there is no evidence to suggest that this would be imminent, particularly in light of the fact that she has given birth to her second child.
The husband alleged in his case outline that the wife had previously indicated an intention to engage in on-call work, which he asserted would yield $3,000 per day or $750,000 per annum. The chronology in his case outline indicated that this suggestion had been made over ten years earlier, in September 2015, when the wife’s circumstances were markedly different. There is no evidence before the court to suggest that she has ever undertaken on-call work, nor that such work would be available to her at all, much less on every working day of the year as the husband’s calculation would appear to suggest.
The husband’s income over the years has been variable. The wife deposed that at times during their relationship when the husband worked on a fly-in/fly-out basis, he earned at least $140,000 per annum, albeit for a short period of time. The husband claimed to be unable to recall having earned this amount but did not dispute that he may have done.
The husband’s Financial Statement indicated that he was not employed and was not in receipt of any income, but as already mentioned, this document was unreliable and the husband’s failure to comply with his disclosure obligations precludes any finding in his favour in this regard.
The husband asserted in his unsworn case outline that his taxable income for the financial year ending 30 June 2025 had been $75,000 and that he would be unable to earn a “six figure salary” without working a roster that was unsuited to family responsibilities. Noting that the husband has undertaken fly-in, fly-out work in the past, including during the course of these proceedings, and that the parenting orders to be made will not inhibit his ability to do so in future, this does not appear to be an unrealistic possibility.
The evidence given by the husband under cross-examination with respect to his employment circumstances was highly unsatisfactory. He was extremely evasive and claimed to be unable to remember even basic details.
His taxation returns for the financial years ended 30 June 2023[118] and 30 June 2024[119] disclose taxable income of $58,539 and $56,749 respectively, though his taxable income was $97,983 in the financial year ending 30 June 2015 and $110,294 in the financial year ending 30 June 2016. He did not disclose his tax returns for any other financial years, though he did assert during his closing submissions (well after the close of his case) that he had belatedly obtained others.
Senior counsel for the wife submitted that limited weight could be placed on the difference in the parties’ earning capacities in circumstances in which the husband had not provided documentary evidence to support his assertions. I accept this submission to the extent that the benefit of any doubt should not be construed in favour of the husband. Nonetheless, it is apparent from the evidence before the Court (including the wife’s own evidence) that her earning capacity substantially exceeds that of the husband and this is a factor to which I have regard.
I have regard also to the fact that the parties’ agreed position that there will be no superannuation split will have the effect that the wife will retain greater superannuation entitlements than will the husband.

(d) the effect of any material wastage, caused intentionally or recklessly by a party to the marriage, of property or financial resources of either of the parties to the marriage or both of them

Despite remaining in the former matrimonial home, the husband made no contributions to the mortgage in March 2022 or between October 2022 and the time of the sale of the property in September 2023, causing the mortgage to fall into arrears of $7,528.48. He also failed to pay water bills for the property from November 2021.

(f) the extent to which either party to the marriage has the care of a child of the marriage who has not attained the age of 18 years, including the need of either party to provide appropriate housing for such a child

The wife will continue to have the full-time care of X, who is a very young child and will be financially dependent upon her for years to come. She works on a part time basis and deposed to the care of X having impacted upon her earning capacity, particularly to the extent that it limits her ability to do “on call” shifts.

(g) commitments of each of the parties to the marriage that are necessary to enable the party to support themselves and any child or other person that the party has a duty to maintain

The wife has the care of the very young child of her current relationship.

(n) the extent to which each party to the marriage has contributed to the income, earning capacity, property and financial resources of the other party

As already discussed, the husband asserted that he had contributed to the wife’s income and earning capacity by moving interstate with her. I accept that he did so, but not that this involved any sacrifice in terms of his own career.

(q) if either party to the marriage is cohabiting with another person—the financial circumstances relating to the cohabitation

Although the wife deposed to being in a committed, long-term relationship with the father of her younger child, and in June 2025, the husband raised with the CCS the prospect of bringing X’s “stepmother”[120] to a supervised visit and gave evidence of being in a relationship with a person named “[Ms U],” the evidence does not suggest that either party is presently cohabiting with another person.

(s) any child support under the Child Support (Assessment) Act 1989 that a party to the marriage is to provide, or might be liable to provide in the future, for a child of the marriage

In circumstances in which the husband has paid no child support for X during the period of over four years since the parties separated, there is no basis for any expectation that he will do so in future.

Other considerations - subsection 79(5)(v)

As already mentioned, shortly prior to the parties’ separation, the wife withdrew the sum of $65,228 from the parties’ home loan, being additional contributions she had made to that loan during the relationship. She deposed to having done so in order to have funds to use for living expenses. She remained on unpaid parental leave and was not in paid employment until she returned to work in early 2022, at which time she worked part-time and in particular, did not work on Mondays or Fridays in order to facilitate the regular travel to City K. It is an undisputed fact that during the months that followed that withdrawal, the wife paid for her own flights and accommodation when she travelled to City K two weekends out of five to facilitate X spending time with the husband. It is also an undisputed fact that the husband was paying no child support.
It is well established (albeit in the context of the law as it previously stood, whereby the practice of notionally “adding back” property was available), that parties are entitled to proceed with their economic lives in a reasonable manner, and will not be held to account for expenditure of funds that existed at the time of separation on their own reasonable maintenance.[121] In all the circumstances, I am not satisfied that it would be just an equitable to make an adjustment against the wife in respect of the funds accessed by her following separation.

Assessment of subsection 79(5) adjustment

The wife contended for an adjustment in her favour of 10 per cent with respect to the factors enumerated in subsection 79(5). The husband’s position was that there should be an adjustment in his favour as a result of the injuries sustained in the 2019 accident and the difference in the parties’ respective earning capacities, though he did not expressly quantify the adjudgment sought.
Having regard to all of the considerations outlined above, I conclude that an adjustment of 10 per cent in favour of the wife on account of subsection 79(5) factors, the most significant of which is her full-time care of X, would be just and equitable. Such an adjustment would result in an overall distribution of the parties’ non-superannuation assets and liabilities in equal proportions.
It is necessary to consider the resulting differential between the parties and what it represents in monetary terms.[122] In the present case, there will be no differential.

Justice and equity and the terms of the orders to be made

The requirement that the court must not make an order unless it is satisfied that, in all the circumstances, it is just and equitable to make the order is the “overriding requirement” of the section 79 process.[123] This requirement includes consideration of the type of order, including the form and structure of the order, to be made. The court is required to “stand back” from its preliminary determination, and consider its impact.[124] It must be satisfied that the actual orders made (and not just the underlying percentage division) is just and equitable.[125]
The equal distribution of the parties’ net non-superannuation assets and liabilities which I have found to be just and equitable may be achieved by a sale of the boat and trailer and distribution of the net proceeds, together with a distribution of the funds held on trust by the parties for the conveyancers being distributed (subject to further adjustments, as discussed below) in proportions of $178,729.91 to the wife and $33,949.09 to the husband. The husband will otherwise retain the Town H property, which was owned by him prior to the parties’ relationship.
In all of the circumstances and having regard to all of the considerations outlined above, I am satisfied that both the percentage-based division and the underlying outcome is just and equitable.

COSTS

On 11 December 2024, an order was made requiring the husband to pay to the wife the sum of $3,977 in costs within 90 days. That costs order remains unpaid. The wife sought that this outstanding payment be adjusted as part of the parties’ overall property settlement. I am satisfied that this is appropriate, and such an adjustment will be made, bringing the total sum to be paid to the wife from the funds held by the conveyancers to $182,706.91.
The ICL sought an order that the husband pay the sum of $5,000, being his contribution to the costs of the ICL. The Court was informed that both parties had been requested to make such a contribution, the wife had done so, and the husband had unsuccessfully sought a waiver but still had not made the requested contribution. The ICL had been instructed by the Legal Services Commission (“LSC”) to request an order for the payment. I accept that it is appropriate that this adjustment be made as sought by the ICL. The orders will provide for this sum to be deducted from the funds which would otherwise have been paid to the husband and will be paid to the LSC on his behalf.
The wife otherwise sought a further order for the costs of the proceedings. That application was not particularised and was not expressly pressed at trial. In my view, it would not, in any event, be appropriate to consider any application for costs prior to the parties having had the opportunity to have regard to these reasons and to adduce evidence of any offers which may have passed between them. Each of the parties will retain their rights to make an application for costs pursuant to subsection 114UB(4)(b) of the Act should they consider it appropriate to do so.

CONCLUSION

For all of the reasons outlined above, I make the orders as set out at the commencement of these reasons.
